The first round of storms moved through during the morning hours while a second round affected the region during the afternoon into the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding over portions of Northwest Fayette County. The intersection of Hwy 196 and Orr Road was flooded as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across Middle Tennessee early on April 27 and continued into the afternoon hours as a weak warm frontal boundary was draped across the region. Some flooding was reported in the Nashville metro area. Additional showers and thunderstorms formed later in the day on April 27 and continued into April 28. Training thunderstorms caused widespread flash flooding. The worst flash flooding was across Houston, Stewart, and Montgomery counties where many homes and businesses were flooded, numerous roads were washed out, and several people were rescued from the flood waters. Several reports of wind damage and large hail were also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A barn and an outbuilding were struck by lightning and set on fire on Turkey McCollum Road in Cornersville.

Other roadways that were flooded included roads near Drakes Creek Park in Hendersonville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain developed along a stalled front across Northeast Arkansas early in the day causing flash flooding. This heavy rain eventually sagged into Northwest Tennessee resulting in some flash flooding there later in the day. As a day progressed a potent upper level disturbance moved out of the East Texas while a surface low pressure system tracked from Arkansas along the stalled front. Moist and unstable air moved into the Mid-South in advance of the stalled front and the developing low pressure system. As a result a line of showers and thunderstorms moved across West Tennessee during the evening hours of December 21st.  Several storms were severe with reports of damaging winds, hail and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked a tree on to a house on Roberts Road between Medon and Pinson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a continuation of an event that started during the late evening hours on Thursday, January 31st, a low pressure system that had swept rapidly southeastward from the Ohio River Valley across the Tennessee Valley during the late evening hours on Thursday, January 31st, bringing with it a band of snow showers that had impacted mainly central portions of Middle Tennessee that evening, began the process of moving  quickly eastward across the remainder of the mid state through the early morning hours on Friday, February 1st, as temperatures fell further into the 20s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four miles south of Sparta, seven tenths of total snowfall accumulation occurred. Also, three miles west northwest of Sparta, one half inch of total snowfall accumulation occurred. This total snowfall accumulation resulted in the closures or delays for private schools and public school systems along with delays or postponements of civic engagements across these areas, with accumulating snowfall on roadways causing a significant impact to commerce and transportation across these areas also.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large scale upper trough sat up over the Upper and Lower Mississippi River Valleys on July 22nd-July 23rd, 2013. Several upper level disturbances rotated around the base of the upper trough. The disturbances set off mesoscale convective complexes during the overnight hours which pushed south during the daytime. The convection would die out during the afternoon hours. This happened on the 22nd and again on the 23rd. The thunderstorms produced damaging winds and very heavy rain which caused flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked numerous trees down in Stantonville. Some trees fell on houses and vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through West Tennessee during the evening of October 5th, 2013. One mini-supercell developed out ahead of the front. The storm produced an brief tornado and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down several trees and power lines in South Fulton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached the Mid-South during the day on October 31st, 2013. Widespread rain occurred through much of the day. However...rain tapered off by late afternoon. There was enough of a window for the atmosphere to recover before the actual cold front moved into the area during the evening. Thus a couple of severe thunderstorms developed ahead of the front during the evening hours across West Tennessee. Damaging winds occurred from the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down trees between Fowlkes and Roellen. HIghway 210 in Fowlkes and Don Hurley Road near Roellen were blocked as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front pushed into Northwest Tennessee during the evening of December 5th, 2013. Widespread rain was already occurring, due to the combination of abundant mid-level moisture, an enhanced upper level jet and an upper level disturbance moving through the area. As a result of the cold front, temperatures plummeted and turned rain over to freezing rain and sleet across Northwest Tennessee.  The wintry precipitation tapered off during the evening hours on December 6th, 2013. Total sleet amounts ranged from just a trace of sleet, roughly from a Memphis to Jackson line, to greater than one and a half inches across Lake County. Just southeast of the heavy sleet accumulations, a corridor of heavy ice accumulations occurred. The corridor, including locations such as Ripley, Dyersburg, Martin and Paris, received greater than a quarter inch of ice. South of this corridor, around a tenth of an inch of ice fell. The wintry precipitation created hazardous travel. However most of the accumulations occurred on trees, power lines, and elevated surfaces. As a result, a few trees and power lines fell due to the ice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of a quarter of an inch of ice and a half inch of sleet fell across Carroll County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain developed along a stalled front across Northeast Arkansas early in the day causing flash flooding. This heavy rain eventually sagged into Northwest Tennessee resulting in some flash flooding there later in the day. As a day progressed a potent upper level disturbance moved out of the East Texas while a surface low pressure system tracked from Arkansas along the stalled front. Moist and unstable air moved into the Mid-South in advance of the stalled front and the developing low pressure system. As a result a line of showers and thunderstorms moved across West Tennessee during the evening hours of December 21st.  Several storms were severe with reports of damaging winds, hail and flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper level trough moved across the United States from Tuesday, January 29th into Wednesday, January 30th. This system contained unusually strong winds aloft, with wind speeds of 80 mph just 2500 feet above the surface and up to 150 mph at 20,000 feet. A record warm airmass with temperatures in the 60s and 70s spread northward ahead of the system into the Tennessee and Ohio Valleys, bringing weak atmospheric instability into the region. As a powerful cold front moved eastward across Middle Tennessee during the early morning hours of January 30, the strong low level jet ahead of the front caused strong gradient winds of 40 to 65 mph at the surface, resulting in several reports of wind damage. A peak sustained south wind of 45 mph with a peak gust of 64 mph was measured by the ASOS at the Nashville International Airport around 315 AM CST in association with these intense gradient winds. A line of showers and thunderstorms known as a Quasi-Linear Convective System (QLCS) developed along the front near the Tennessee River and raced eastward through the area between 2 AM and 6 AM, producing at least 23 tornadoes and widespread wind damage. This severe weather event resulted in 1 fatality and at least 3 injuries across the Mid State. The total of 23 tornadoes made the January 30, 2013 event the largest January tornado outbreak in Middle Tennessee history.  It also made January 30, 2013 the second biggest outbreak of tornadoes for any month in Middle Tennessee history.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado touched down in Ashland City near Highway 12 and Batson Street, them moved eastward before lifting east of Floyd Temple Road.  A few homes suffered EF0 damage on Batson Street and Oak Street, and one girl was injured on Oak Street when a window of her house blew in on her.  An apartment complex suffered minor roof and interior water damage on Oak Circle. More homes suffered minor damage from Valley View Street to Bell Street.  EF1 damage occurred between Bell Street and Floyd Temple Road where hundreds of trees were snapped and uprooted before the tornado lifted east of Floyd Temple Road. This tornado along with straight line winds to its south damaged a total of 46 buildings in Ashland Cty according to Cheatham County Emergency Management, including 37 houses, two churches and three apartment buildings, and left two homes uninhabitable. This entry was significantly updated in January 2015 based on aerial and street imagery from Google Earth.

The corridor, including locations such as Ripley, Dyersburg, Martin and Paris, received greater than a quarter inch of ice. South of this corridor, around a tenth of an inch of ice fell. The wintry precipitation created hazardous travel. However most of the accumulations occurred on trees, power lines, and elevated surfaces. As a result, a few trees and power lines fell due to the ice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of a quarter of an inch of ice and around one and a half inches of sleet fell across Obion County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain developed along a stalled front across Northeast Arkansas early in the day causing flash flooding. This heavy rain eventually sagged into Northwest Tennessee resulting in some flash flooding there later in the day. As a day progressed a potent upper level disturbance moved out of the East Texas while a surface low pressure system tracked from Arkansas along the stalled front. Moist and unstable air moved into the Mid-South in advance of the stalled front and the developing low pressure system. As a result a line of showers and thunderstorms moved across West Tennessee during the evening hours of December 21st.  Several storms were severe with reports of damaging winds, hail and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over four inches of rain produced flash flooding across Obion County. Widespread street flooding occurred in Union City and Highway 183 was flooded in Eastern Obion County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The local atmosphere was largely capped keeping stronger updrafts isolated across the Great Valley and Cumberland Plateau. One storm formed over Hamilton County early in the afternoon generating a brief, severe wind gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three trees were reported down in Soddy Daisy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough and associated cold front moved across the Eastern U.S. bringing a modified Arctic air mass to the region. A trailing short wave trough moved across the Southern Appalachians during the late night hours generating snowfall amounts around one to three inches in the lower terrain and amounts between 3 and 6 inches in the higher terrain areas across extreme Eastern Tennessee and the higher peaks across Southwest Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall acumulated to a depth of 4 inches near the Cherokee National Forest along highway 70 near the North Carolina state line.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were reported down in the community of Hilham, TN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms producing severe straight line winds traversed Middle Tennessee the evening of October 31st and into the early morning on November 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were reported down in the city of Livingston, TN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms producing severe straight line winds traversed Middle Tennessee the evening of October 31st and into the early morning on November 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Putnam County Emergency Manager measured a wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of light freezing drizzle and rain developed during the pre-dawn hours in southern middle Tennessee just in advance of a cold front.  Up to a tenth of an inch of ice accumulated on elevated surfaces in Moore and Franklin Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulation of around a tenth of an inch was reported on area roads around Lynchburg, Tennessee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in middle Tennessee and dropped southward into southern middle Tennessee during the early evening hours.  Isolated wind damage occurred in Lincoln County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds knocked down several large 100-year old trees between Flintville School Road and Red House Road near Elora.  The back of a shed was blown out around this area.  Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system developed over Kansas and Missouri and eventually moved southeast into Eastern Arkansas and the Memphis Metropolitan area during the early morning hours of June 28, 2013. A few storms were severe with damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked a tree on to a truck in the Raleigh area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system developed over Kansas and Missouri and eventually moved southeast into Eastern Arkansas and the Memphis Metropolitan area during the early morning hours of June 28, 2013. A few storms were severe with damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down a couple of large trees and blocked Macon Road about one mile east of Highway 196.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms produced heavy rain which caused flash flooding in portions of West Tennessee on June 10th, 2013.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding across Northern Henderson County. Numerous roads were flooded as a result including Palmer Road, Holmes Road, Rock Milam Road, Spencer Creek Road and Hamlett Road. One water rescue occurred as a result of the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system moved across the Great Lakes while dragging a strong cold front into the Southern Appalachians. Strong winds aloft were brought down to the surface as the front moved through the region generating only isolated reports of wind damage due to the stability of the near surface air mass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An awning was ripped off a building due to the strong wind gust.  Also, windows were broken and two trees were reported down along Manufacturers road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system began to approach the Mid-South during the afternoon and evening hours of June 9th, 2013. A couple of showers and thunderstorms developed due to the combination of peak heating and the upper low. One storm produced hail.

An upper level disturbance moved through the Mid-South during the afternoon and evening hours of June 19th, 2013. The combination of peak heating and the disturbance moving through produced a few showers and thunderstorms. A few storms became severe producing damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked four power poles down near the intersection of Macon Road and Berryhill Road. Several trees and fences were also knocked down in the area. Tree branches were knocked down as far west as the intersection of Germantown Road and Macon Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very hot and unstable airmass prevailed across the Mid-South during the afternoon of June 27, 2013. A weak surface boundary was stretched from Northeast Arkansas into North Mississippi.  A few thunderstorms developed along this boundary in Northeast Arkansas during the late afternoon and eventually formed a mesoscale convective system that traveled southeast across the Memphis Metropolitan area into Northwest Mississippi. A few severe storms occurred with large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked a large tree on to a house on East Dempster Avenue in Memphis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified as it moved northeast along a stationary front stretching from Southwest Arkansas into the Ohio Valley. Deep moisture surged north from the Gulf of Mexico resulting in a large area of heavy showers and thunderstorms moving into western sections of the Mid-South during the evening of January 12, 2013. The showers and thunderstorms spread slowly east across the remainder of the Mid-South in advance of a cold front through the afternoon of January 13, 2013. The system dropped up to five inches of rain in isolated locations. The heavy rainfall resulted in some flash flooding and areal flooding across parts of the Mid-South.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced areal flooding across parts of Chester County. Several roads were closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified as it moved northeast along a stationary front stretching from Southwest Arkansas into the Ohio Valley. Deep moisture surged north from the Gulf of Mexico resulting in a large area of heavy showers and thunderstorms moving into western sections of the Mid-South during the evening of January 12, 2013. The showers and thunderstorms spread slowly east across the remainder of the Mid-South in advance of a cold front through the afternoon of January 13, 2013. The system dropped up to five inches of rain in isolated locations. The heavy rainfall resulted in some flash flooding and areal flooding across parts of the Mid-South.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced areal flooding over parts of Madison County. Christmasville Road was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying surface low moved from the Southern Plains into the Great Lakes. Meanwhile the associated strong cold front swept across the Mid-South during the evening hours of January 29, 2013 into the early morning hours of January 30, 2013. Strong winds aloft combined with an increasingly unstable airmass set the stage for severe weather across the region. A broken line of thunderstorms moved into Eastern Arkansas during the evening hours of January 29, 2013 and eventually formed a solid line of thunderstorms as it crossed the Mississippi River and moved into West Tennessee and North Mississippi during the overnight hours.  There were numerous reports of damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds ahead of a cold front knocked down several trees near Paris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much colder air filtered into the region behind a strong cold front. This shallow cold air mass, combined with over-running precipitation across the area, resulted in periods of freezing rain across portions of southern middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain was occurring with around a tenth of an inch of icing estimated to have occurred with a slight glaze of ice reported on most elevated objects, about six miles south of Fayetteville Tennessee near Park City, Tennessee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying surface low moved from the Southern Plains into the Great Lakes. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down several large trees in the University of Memphis area. A very large business sign was knocked over on Germantown Parkway in Cordova. The ASOS at Memphis International Airport reported a gust of 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread rain with a few embedded thunderstorms spread across Middle Tennessee during the evening hours of February 23. Lightning from one storm in Humphreys County reportedly struck a food building at the Loretta Lynn Ranch in Hurricane Mills, and the resulting fire destroyed the structure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning reportedly struck a food building at the Loretta Lynn Ranch in Hurricane Mills, and the resulting fire destroyed the structure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of snowfall moved into Middle Tennessee during the early morning hours of February 2 and spread across the area into the morning hours on February 3. Temperatures warmed above freezing and changed the snow over to rain across much of Middle Tennessee during the day on February 2, but the Cumberland Plateau area remained below freezing and no changeover occurred.  Snowfall amounts up to 2 inches were reported in several area, with significant impacts to travel due to snow on roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports from Facebook indicated 1 to 2 inches of snow across Williamson County, with the heaviest amounts in Fairview and Brentwood. This snow resulted in significant impacts to travel across the county, with several road closures including major roadways such as Interstate 65 and Highway 840.

Snowfall amounts up to 2 inches were reported in several area, with significant impacts to travel due to snow on roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS employees reported up to 1 inch of snow accumulation across Davidson County. This snow resulted in significant impacts to travel, with some roadways closed due to being snow covered and icy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system moved Across the Upper Ohio Valley and Central Appalachians late Sunday night into Monday while pulling significantly colder air into the Southern Appalachian region. Strong northwesterly winds produced a significant amount of lift over the higher terrain across Southwest Virginia and Northeast Tennessee as well as the Smoky Mountains. Snowfall totals for the two day period of upslope wind flow ranged from 8 to 12 inches across the higher terrain over Southwest Virginia and Northeast Tennessee while the highest peaks of the Smoky Mountains measured snowfall amounts as high as 12 to 18 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten inches of snow was measured at Flag Pond.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of snowfall moved into Middle Tennessee during the early morning hours of February 2 and spread across the area into the morning hours on February 3. Temperatures warmed above freezing and changed the snow over to rain across much of Middle Tennessee during the day on February 2, but the Cumberland Plateau area remained below freezing and no changeover occurred.  Snowfall amounts up to 2 inches were reported in several area, with significant impacts to travel due to snow on roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management and CoCoRaHS reports indicated one to two inches of snow accumulation across Fentress County. This snow resulted in significant impacts to travel due to snow covered and icy roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying surface low moved from the Southern Plains into the Great Lakes. Meanwhile the associated strong cold front swept across the Mid-South during the evening hours of January 29, 2013 into the early morning hours of January 30, 2013. Strong winds aloft combined with an increasingly unstable airmass set the stage for severe weather across the region. A broken line of thunderstorms moved into Eastern Arkansas during the evening hours of January 29, 2013 and eventually formed a solid line of thunderstorms as it crossed the Mississippi River and moved into West Tennessee and North Mississippi during the overnight hours.  There were numerous reports of damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding across parts of Memphis. The Poplar Avenue and International Place intersection was flooded in East Memphis. Airways Road and Swinnea Road near Memphis International were flooded. Vehicles were stuck in deep water near Walnut Grove Road and Interstate 240 in East Memphis. Water was as deep as the windows of a stalled car at Timber Creek Drive and Walnut Bend Road in Cordova.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front tracked through the region producing high non-convective mountain waves over mainly the higher elevations.  Wind gusts of 74 mph were measured at the Cove Mountain wind tower near Gatlinburg.  In addition a wind gust of 72 mph was measured at the Camp Creek Elementary School in Camp Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 72 mph was measured at the NOAA wind tower at Camp Creek Elementary School in Camp Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Post-frontal rain lingered across West Tennessee near the Tennessee River due to an upper level disturbance that was moving through the region. The rain fell for 12 hours. Three to four inches of rain fell across Henderson, Carroll and Decatur Counties during that time span. Low water crossings were flooded as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water covered the roadway at a low water crossing on Highway 219 and Rosser Circle Road, northeast of Huntingdon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snow fell across the higher terrain in Southwest Virginia and Northeast Tennessee as well as the Smoky Mountains from Tuesday evening through Wednesday afternoon. The heavy snow event was the result of the passage of a deep upper level low pressure system. Much of these higher terrain areas picked up three to six inches of snow while the highest peaks in the Smoky Mountains recorded snow totals as high as ten to fifteen inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around two inches of snow was reported at Oneida.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High shear along with limited instability resulted in isolated severe thunderstorm development across Southeast Tennessee. An area of low pressure moving northeast along the Ohio Valley pulled a cold front into the Southern Appalachians. A strong low level jet the was in place ahead of the front helped to strengthen convective gusts in the storms producing wind damage in a few spots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were reported down around the Chattanooga area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved through Middle Tennessee into Northeast Tennessee during the early evening hours. A National Weather Service survey team confirmed a tornado was embedded in a bowing line segment that moved across the south part of New Tazewell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Weather Service meteorologists surveyed a short track of tornado damage that began about one mile south of New Tazewell and ended just east of the Claiborne County High School.  The wind speeds were estimated to range from 70 mph to 105 mph.  The narrow path of damage contained numerous mature cedar tree trunks snapped or uprooted along the entire path.  No noticeable roof damage occurred.  However, some property damage was sustained when tree tops slammed into homes or downed power lines.  One of the buses in the back parking lot of the high school had its windows blown out toward the end of the tornado track.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified as it moved northeast along a stationary front stretching from Southwest Arkansas into the Ohio Valley. Deep moisture surged north from the Gulf of Mexico resulting in a large area of heavy showers and thunderstorms moving into western sections of the Mid-South during the evening of January 12, 2013. The showers and thunderstorms spread slowly east across the remainder of the Mid-South in advance of a cold front through the afternoon of January 13, 2013. The system dropped up to five inches of rain in isolated locations. The heavy rainfall resulted in some flash flooding and areal flooding across parts of the Mid-South.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in flash flooding across Decatur County. Thirteen roads were closed throughout the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall produced flash flooding in Crockett County. Six inches of water covered  Highway 152 and other roads throughout the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough and an associated cold front moved into the Lower Mississippi Valley during the evening of May 21st, 2013. Several rounds of showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the system affecting the Mid-South. Some storms became severe producing large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding. The first round of storms moved through during the morning hours while a second round affected the region during the afternoon into the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding over much of Shelby County. Memphis International Airport received 4.8 inches of rain over a span of a couple of hours. Interstate 55 near 3rd Street near Downtown Memphis was closed as a result of the flooding. Flooding was also reported near Macon Road and Houston Levee Road in Cordova, Brunswick Road and Old Brownsville Road in Northern Shelby County. Two people were trapped in a flooded car at Lamar and East Shelby Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moving through the Mid-South interacted with a moist and unstable airmass to produce scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours of May 30, 2013 . A few of the storms became severe with large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A swath of sporadic straight line wind damage stretched across Madison County. The damage started south of Bemis continued into Jackson then extended north roughly between Old Medina Road and Christmasville Road to the Gibson County line. Numerous trees were knocked down with the most intense damage north of North Parkway in Jackson. A large tree fell on a house in Bemis causing extensive damage. Another large tree fell and destroyed a barber shop in Bemis. Numerous trees were knocked down on Hickory Hollow Drive east of the center of Jackson where one tree fell and damaged a porch of a home. A middle school roof in Jackson was damaged. A tree damaged a home in North Jackson. A metal barn and outbuilding on Christmasville road were destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon across Middle Tennessee. One person was reportedly struck by lightning in Nashville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One person was reportedly struck by lightning and injured in Sylvan Park. Reported via tspotter on Twitter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across much of Middle Tennessee from the late morning hours into the evening on June 9. Several reports of large hail and damaging winds were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down and blocking Happy Mountain Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low moved southeastward across Middle Tennessee during the afternoon hours on June 10. Moderate wind shear combined with the cold mid level temperatures with the upper level low to produce numerous supercell thunderstorms and small bow echoes across mainly central portions of Middle Tennessee, with widespread wind damage and a few large hail reports. Many of the supercell thunderstorms had spectacular visual structure with well-defined wall clouds and shelf clouds. This was the largest severe weather event to affect Middle Tennessee in the meteorological Summer of 2013.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down along Highway 53 about 5 miles north of Manchester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An mesoscale convective system developed over Southern Missouri and Northern Arkansas and pushed across Northeast Arkansas into West Tennessee during the early morning hours of May 31, 2013.  A few severe storms produced damaging winds and flash flooding. A couple of weak tornadoes also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds produced damaged to a couple of homes northwest of Covington.  The homes had roof and some structural damage.

This was the largest severe weather event to affect Middle Tennessee in the meteorological Summer of 2013.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several barns and outbuildings were damaged in Summitville with numerous trees blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough and an associated cold front moved into the Lower Mississippi Valley during the evening of May 21st, 2013. The first round of storms moved through during the morning hours while a second round affected the region during the afternoon into the evening.

Bartlett was particularly hit hard as up to 3 feet of water covered portions of Stage Road. Vehicles were stuck in water. Further west, the intersection of Walnut Grove Road and Union Avenue was flooded with up to 4 feet of water in Midtown Memphis. In addition, the intersection of Macon Road and Jackson Avenue in North Memphis was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper level trough moved across the United States from Tuesday, January 29th into Wednesday, January 30th. This system contained unusually strong winds aloft, with wind speeds of 80 mph just 2500 feet above the surface and up to 150 mph at 20,000 feet. A record warm airmass with temperatures in the 60s and 70s spread northward ahead of the system into the Tennessee and Ohio Valleys, bringing weak atmospheric instability into the region. As a powerful cold front moved eastward across Middle Tennessee during the early morning hours of January 30, the strong low level jet ahead of the front caused strong gradient winds of 40 to 65 mph at the surface, resulting in several reports of wind damage. A peak sustained south wind of 45 mph with a peak gust of 64 mph was measured by the ASOS at the Nashville International Airport around 315 AM CST in association with these intense gradient winds. A line of showers and thunderstorms known as a Quasi-Linear Convective System (QLCS) developed along the front near the Tennessee River and raced eastward through the area between 2 AM and 6 AM, producing at least 23 tornadoes and widespread wind damage. This severe weather event resulted in 1 fatality and at least 3 injuries across the Mid State. The total of 23 tornadoes made the January 30, 2013 event the largest January tornado outbreak in Middle Tennessee history.  It also made January 30, 2013 the second biggest outbreak of tornadoes for any month in Middle Tennessee history.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF2 tornado began southwest of the Coble community then moved northeast along Highway 438, snapping and uprooting hundreds of trees on both sides of the highway. Damage became much more significant in the center of Coble around the intersection of Briar Pond Road and Highway 438. Several brick homes suffered major damage in Coble including one with almost total roof loss and the collapse of a wall. A mobile home was completely destroyed with debris scattered and the metal frame wrapped partially around a tree. A cinder block garage and at least 3 barns were demolished and an RV was flipped on its side. There were no injuries. The tornado then curved eastward and crossed Sulphur Creek Road, Beaverdam Road, and Highway 50, blowing down hundreds of more trees. The tornado lifted just east of Smithfield Road. Aerial and street view imagery from Google Earth greatly assisted with this storm survey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low moved southeastward across Middle Tennessee during the afternoon hours on June 10. Moderate wind shear combined with the cold mid level temperatures with the upper level low to produce numerous supercell thunderstorms and small bow echoes across mainly central portions of Middle Tennessee, with widespread wind damage and a few large hail reports. Many of the supercell thunderstorms had spectacular visual structure with well-defined wall clouds and shelf clouds. This was the largest severe weather event to affect Middle Tennessee in the meteorological Summer of 2013.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down in downtown Nashville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper level trough moved across the United States from Tuesday, January 29th into Wednesday, January 30th. This system contained unusually strong winds aloft, with wind speeds of 80 mph just 2500 feet above the surface and up to 150 mph at 20,000 feet. A record warm airmass with temperatures in the 60s and 70s spread northward ahead of the system into the Tennessee and Ohio Valleys, bringing weak atmospheric instability into the region. As a powerful cold front moved eastward across Middle Tennessee during the early morning hours of January 30, the strong low level jet ahead of the front caused strong gradient winds of 40 to 65 mph at the surface, resulting in several reports of wind damage. A peak sustained south wind of 45 mph with a peak gust of 64 mph was measured by the ASOS at the Nashville International Airport around 315 AM CST in association with these intense gradient winds. A line of showers and thunderstorms known as a Quasi-Linear Convective System (QLCS) developed along the front near the Tennessee River and raced eastward through the area between 2 AM and 6 AM, producing at least 23 tornadoes and widespread wind damage. This severe weather event resulted in 1 fatality and at least 3 injuries across the Mid State. The total of 23 tornadoes made the January 30, 2013 event the largest January tornado outbreak in Middle Tennessee history.  It also made January 30, 2013 the second biggest outbreak of tornadoes for any month in Middle Tennessee history.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong south winds ahead of an approaching squall line caused the second floor of an old building to collapse on Depot Street in downtown Lawrenceburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved through the Mid-South January 15th into January 16th, 2013. Temperatures remained near or below freezing following another upper level disturbance that moved through on January 14th, 2013. As a result, precipitation from the disturbance that moved through on January 15th and 16th fell predominately in the form of freezing rain. Ice accumulations of a tenth to a quarter inch fell in many locations. However, most of the accumulations occurred on trees and power lines. Some of which were knocked down. Roads remained wet due to warm ground temperatures. Only bridges and overpasses became iced over due to them being elevated. In addition, colder air filtered into extreme Northwest Tennessee as the disturbance pushed east. This allowed freezing rain to change over to snow briefly before ending. Snow accumulations up to an inch occurred across Lake County. Some roads became snow covered as a result producing hazardous travel. One car accident occurred which produced one minor injury.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Less than an inch snow accumulation fell on top of ice accumulations around a tenth of an inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small but intense area of low pressure moved northeastward across the Cumberland Plateau during the early morning hours of February 26. Strong southeast winds measured at 40 to 60 mph caused considerable wind damage across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crossville ASOS measured a peak wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a plume of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico continued streaming northeastward into Middle Tennessee during the afternoon hours on Tuesday, January 15th through the early morning hours on Wednesday, January 16th, and with surface temperatures near freezing, freezing rain that fell resulted in significant ice accumulations of between eight hundreths to one quarter of an inch for locations generally around and west of Interstate 65 through the Tennessee River Valley Region of Middle Tennessee. This resulted in a significant impact to commerce and transportation across this portion of the mid state, including several automobile accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lots of tree limbs were downed across the county due to ice accumulations on them of one tenth of an inch. This resulted in a significant impact to commerce and transportation across the county from the evening hours on Tuesday, January 15th through the early morning hours on Wednesday, January 16th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulations around two tenths of an inch fell across Madison County. One car accident occurred which produced one minor injury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a continuation of an event that started during the late evening hours on Thursday, January 31st, a low pressure system that had swept rapidly southeastward from the Ohio River Valley across the Tennessee Valley during the late evening hours on Thursday, January 31st, bringing with it a band of snow showers that had impacted mainly central portions of Middle Tennessee that evening, began the process of moving  quickly eastward across the remainder of the mid state through the early morning hours on Friday, February 1st, as temperatures fell further into the 20s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wilder reported two inches of total snowfall accumulation. Three miles south south east of Jamestown, one an one tenth of an inch of total snowfall accumulation occurred. This total snowfall accumulation resulted in the closures or delays for private schools and public school systems along with delays or postponements of civic engagements across this area, with accumulating snowfall on roadways causing a significant impact to commerce and transportation across this area also.

As a powerful cold front moved eastward across Middle Tennessee during the early morning hours of January 30, the strong low level jet ahead of the front caused strong gradient winds of 40 to 65 mph at the surface, resulting in several reports of wind damage. A peak sustained south wind of 45 mph with a peak gust of 64 mph was measured by the ASOS at the Nashville International Airport around 315 AM CST in association with these intense gradient winds. A line of showers and thunderstorms known as a Quasi-Linear Convective System (QLCS) developed along the front near the Tennessee River and raced eastward through the area between 2 AM and 6 AM, producing at least 23 tornadoes and widespread wind damage. This severe weather event resulted in 1 fatality and at least 3 injuries across the Mid State. The total of 23 tornadoes made the January 30, 2013 event the largest January tornado outbreak in Middle Tennessee history.  It also made January 30, 2013 the second biggest outbreak of tornadoes for any month in Middle Tennessee history.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were reported downed in Fairview.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS Storm Survey determined a very complex area of wind damage consisting of two EF1 tornadoes and a large intense macroburst struck eastern Robertson County. The first of these EF1 tornadoes touched down 4.6 miles southeast of Springfield west of Pinson School Road and traveled northeast across Possum Trot Road uprooting and snapping several trees. More significant damage to homes and businesses occurred at the intersection of Highway 76 and Gum Station Road and along Lorilei Lane, where a few homes had moderate roof damage and many more homes and barns had minor roof damage. More homes and barns received minor damage south of Gum Station Road, with one home at 6537 Gum Station Road having moderate roof damage to its southern side and a tree falling on the western side. Another home at the southwest corner of the Bethlehem Road and Gum Station Road intersection was severely damaged, with others on the north side of the intersection suffering minor damage. More homes had minor roof damage further to the east along Bethlehem Road and at the Cross Plains Road and Boyles Road intersection before the tornado lifted. Hundreds of trees were snapped and uprooted all along the path. Winds were estimated up to 105 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a plume of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico continued streaming northeastward into Middle Tennessee during the afternoon hours on Tuesday, January 15th through the early morning hours on Wednesday, January 16th, and with surface temperatures near freezing, freezing rain that fell resulted in significant ice accumulations of between eight hundreths to one quarter of an inch for locations generally around and west of Interstate 65 through the Tennessee River Valley Region of Middle Tennessee. This resulted in a significant impact to commerce and transportation across this portion of the mid state, including several automobile accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near Pegram, several trees were downed due to freezing rain accumulations of one tenth of an inch at the intersection of Sams Creek Road and U.S. Highway 70.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of snowfall moved into Middle Tennessee during the early morning hours of February 2 and spread across the area into the morning hours on February 3. Temperatures warmed above freezing and changed the snow over to rain across much of Middle Tennessee during the day on February 2, but the Cumberland Plateau area remained below freezing and no changeover occurred.  Snowfall amounts up to 2 inches were reported in several area, with significant impacts to travel due to snow on roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports from Twitter indicated up to 1 inch of snow accumulation across Wilson County. This snow resulted in significant impacts to travel due to snow covered and icy roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved through the Mid-South January 15th into January 16th, 2013. Temperatures remained near or below freezing following another upper level disturbance that moved through on January 14th, 2013. As a result, precipitation from the disturbance that moved through on January 15th and 16th fell predominately in the form of freezing rain. Ice accumulations of a tenth to a quarter inch fell in many locations. However, most of the accumulations occurred on trees and power lines. Some of which were knocked down. Roads remained wet due to warm ground temperatures. Only bridges and overpasses became iced over due to them being elevated. In addition, colder air filtered into extreme Northwest Tennessee as the disturbance pushed east. This allowed freezing rain to change over to snow briefly before ending. Snow accumulations up to an inch occurred across Lake County. Some roads became snow covered as a result producing hazardous travel. One car accident occurred which produced one minor injury.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulations around a tenth of an inch fell across Dyer County.

A peak sustained south wind of 45 mph with a peak gust of 64 mph was measured by the ASOS at the Nashville International Airport around 315 AM CST in association with these intense gradient winds. A line of showers and thunderstorms known as a Quasi-Linear Convective System (QLCS) developed along the front near the Tennessee River and raced eastward through the area between 2 AM and 6 AM, producing at least 23 tornadoes and widespread wind damage. This severe weather event resulted in 1 fatality and at least 3 injuries across the Mid State. The total of 23 tornadoes made the January 30, 2013 event the largest January tornado outbreak in Middle Tennessee history.  It also made January 30, 2013 the second biggest outbreak of tornadoes for any month in Middle Tennessee history.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS Storm Survey found a large and intense 2-mile wide macroburst struck areas of eastern Robertson County. Damage began along Armstrong Road east of Springfield where several trees were blown down. More widespread and significant damage began further east along Bethlehem Road and Baggett Road where dozens of homes and outbuildings suffered minor to moderate roof damage and two large barns were destroyed. One 26 year old woman was seriously injured on Baggett Road when a tree fell onto her mobile home. Numerous other homes and mobile homes received minor roof and exterior damage and dozens of trees were snapped or uprooted along Chowning Road, Hoof and Paw Trail, Cook Road, Owens Chapel Road, and Cross Plains Road. Damaged continued eastward with dozens of more homes having minor roof damage, several outbuildings being damaged or destroyed, and many additional trees being snapped or uprooted along Covington Road, Jernigan Road, Apollo Drive, Pleasant Grove Road, and Calista Road. Winds were estimated between 80 and 100 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a low pressure system swept rapidly southeastward from the Ohio River Valley across the Tennessee Valley during the late evening hours on Thursday, January 31st, a band of snow showers quickly entered western and central portions of Middle Tennessee, as temperatures began falling into the 20s. This resulted in a significant impact to commerce and transportation across central portions of the mid state during the late evening hours. This event continued into the mid morning hours on Friday, February 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three miles north of Lebanon, one inch of total snowfall accumulation had occurred with snowfall ending. This resulted in a significant impact to commerce and transportation in and around this area during the late evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved through the Mid-South January 15th into January 16th, 2013. Temperatures remained near or below freezing following another upper level disturbance that moved through on January 14th, 2013. As a result, precipitation from the disturbance that moved through on January 15th and 16th fell predominately in the form of freezing rain. Ice accumulations of a tenth to a quarter inch fell in many locations. However, most of the accumulations occurred on trees and power lines. Some of which were knocked down. Roads remained wet due to warm ground temperatures. Only bridges and overpasses became iced over due to them being elevated. In addition, colder air filtered into extreme Northwest Tennessee as the disturbance pushed east. This allowed freezing rain to change over to snow briefly before ending. Snow accumulations up to an inch occurred across Lake County. Some roads became snow covered as a result producing hazardous travel. One car accident occurred which produced one minor injury.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulations around a tenth of an inch fell across Lauderdale County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and intense squall line moved across Middle Tennessee during the evening hours on December 21 and early morning hours on December 22, producing widespread wind damage in nearly every county of Middle Tennessee. Numerous trees and power lines were blown down with a few reports of damage to homes and businesses. Winds were measured up to 65 mph, and estimated as high as 70 mph in other areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down across Lewis County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF2 tornado touched down south of Mount Zion Road east of Coopertown and moved northeastward before lifting about 4.3 miles southeast of Springfield. A few homes had minor roof damage south of Mount Zion Road and several trees were blown down. A gas station roof was partially blown off and gas pump awning damaged on Mount Zion Road at Tom Austin Highway. On the east side of the highway, an outbuilding was destroyed with debris blown 100 yards to the north into a water tower. One home and a business received roof damage on Old Highway 431, and an adjacent warehouse had an exterior door blown in and half of the roof blown off. Sheet metal from the warehouse was scattered up to 200 yards across adjacent fields. Further to the east, several trees were snapped or uprooted on New Cut Road. Major damage occurred on Stacy Springs road where a TVA high transmission tower was toppled, several homes and outbuildings suffered moderate roof damage, and dozens of trees were snapped and uprooted. Several businesses and barns also had roof damage on Highway 11. More barns and homes were damaged and numerous trees blown down on Old Greenbrier Pike and Worsham Springs Road. Winds were estimated around 120 mph.

A peak sustained south wind of 45 mph with a peak gust of 64 mph was measured by the ASOS at the Nashville International Airport around 315 AM CST in association with these intense gradient winds. A line of showers and thunderstorms known as a Quasi-Linear Convective System (QLCS) developed along the front near the Tennessee River and raced eastward through the area between 2 AM and 6 AM, producing at least 23 tornadoes and widespread wind damage. This severe weather event resulted in 1 fatality and at least 3 injuries across the Mid State. The total of 23 tornadoes made the January 30, 2013 event the largest January tornado outbreak in Middle Tennessee history.  It also made January 30, 2013 the second biggest outbreak of tornadoes for any month in Middle Tennessee history.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dozen reports of trees and power lines downed across western Davidson County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Weather Service Nashville personnel and Wilson County Emergency Management confirmed an EF2 tornado touched down in Mount Juliet on Glenwood Drive, then moved east-northeast through the city before lifting near Cooks Church Road. The heaviest damage occurred in a commercial district around Lebanon Road and Mount Juliet Road, where a Dollar General store and an automotive warehouse were heavily damaged. A distribution warehouse for the Tennessean newspaper on Lineberry Boulevard was nearly completely destroyed, and the roof, third story, and several walls of an adjacent three-story building were blown off.  Dozens of other businesses in the area received minor to moderate structural damage and debris impacts from nearby damaged buildings. Significant damage to outbuildings, fences, and power poles also occurred at the Mount Juilet Ballpark. Dozens of trees and power lines were blown down and many homes received minor damage along the path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a low pressure system swept rapidly southeastward from the Ohio River Valley across the Tennessee Valley during the late evening hours on Thursday, January 31st, a band of snow showers quickly entered western and central portions of Middle Tennessee, as temperatures began falling into the 20s. This resulted in a significant impact to commerce and transportation across central portions of the mid state during the late evening hours. This event continued into the mid morning hours on Friday, February 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Putnam County EMA reported one half inch of total snowfall accumulation in Cookeville and also on average across the remainder of the county with snow still falling. This resulted in a significant impact to commerce and transportation in and around Cookeville and the remaining portions of the county through the late evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved through the Mid-South January 15th into January 16th, 2013. Temperatures remained near or below freezing following another upper level disturbance that moved through on January 14th, 2013. As a result, precipitation from the disturbance that moved through on January 15th and 16th fell predominately in the form of freezing rain. Ice accumulations of a tenth to a quarter inch fell in many locations. However, most of the accumulations occurred on trees and power lines. Some of which were knocked down. Roads remained wet due to warm ground temperatures. Only bridges and overpasses became iced over due to them being elevated. In addition, colder air filtered into extreme Northwest Tennessee as the disturbance pushed east. This allowed freezing rain to change over to snow briefly before ending. Snow accumulations up to an inch occurred across Lake County. Some roads became snow covered as a result producing hazardous travel. One car accident occurred which produced one minor injury.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulations up to two tenths of an inch fell across Fayette County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and intense squall line moved across Middle Tennessee during the evening hours on December 21 and early morning hours on December 22, producing widespread wind damage in nearly every county of Middle Tennessee. Numerous trees and power lines were blown down with a few reports of damage to homes and businesses. Winds were measured up to 65 mph, and estimated as high as 70 mph in other areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A sign was blown out at a Sonic Drive-In in Carthage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado moved back into southern Trousdale County from northern Wilson County for the second time. The tornado moved across Puryears Bend Road and Belcher Lane before crossing the Cumberland River for the fourth time. The tornado finally lifted after crossing Hartsville Pike north of Riadon Road. Dozens of trees were snapped or uprooted along the path with some minor damage to homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a surface trough developed across Middle Tennessee during the morning hours on Friday, January 25th, it became a focusing mechanism for feezing rain development. This precipitation intially began as freezing rain, transitioning to rain from southern portions of the mid state to northern portions as the mid morning hours progressed. Freezing rain left ice accumulations generally around one to two tenths of an inch, resulting in numerous automobile accidents along with several trees and power poles being downed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Secondary roads were icy with bridges and overpasses on these roads also ice covered with estimated ice accumulation due to freezing rain of 0.10 inch. Some residents trying to turn their cars into their driveways on occasions slide past them and their cars became stuck in ditches, resulting in minor accidents. Trees were downed in the Maryland Community and also across State Highway 68 in the southeastern part of the county due to these ice accumulations. A power line was also reported downed in the Tansi area also due to these ice accumulations. Specific details concerning how many, amount and type of damage, associated repair costs, and if any injuries occurred, associated with these accidents, were unknown.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado touched down near the intersection of Highway 31 and Shute Lane in Hendersonville then traveled across southern areas of Gallatin before lifting near Bledsoe Creek State Park. Hundreds of trees were snapped or uprooted all along the path with some trees falling on homes and outbuildings. The roof and one side of a mobile home was blown off near the beginning of the path. Several homes had minor roof damage on Drayton Downs, Gordon Crossing, and Nogs Garden roads. The most significant damage occurred in the Fairview Plantation and Baypoint subdivisions where dozens of homes suffered roof and siding damage on Jacobs Drive, Chloe Drive, Browns Lane, Elizabeth Court, Woodlake Drive, Willowlake Drive, and Inneswood Drive. More homes were damaged on Aqua Drive and Lock 4 Road, and several docks at the Gallatin Marina were damaged. Additional minor roof damage occurred to homes along the path until the tornado lifted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a plume of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico continued streaming northeastward into Middle Tennessee during the afternoon hours on Tuesday, January 15th through the early morning hours on Wednesday, January 16th, and with surface temperatures near freezing, freezing rain that fell resulted in significant ice accumulations of between eight hundreths to one quarter of an inch for locations generally around and west of Interstate 65 through the Tennessee River Valley Region of Middle Tennessee. This resulted in a significant impact to commerce and transportation across this portion of the mid state, including several automobile accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 1/4 inch of ice accumulated near the Cheatham County line in far southeastern Montgomery County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a plume of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico continued streaming northeastward into Middle Tennessee during the afternoon hours on Tuesday, January 15th through the early morning hours on Wednesday, January 16th, and with surface temperatures near freezing, freezing rain that fell resulted in significant ice accumulations of between eight hundreths to one quarter of an inch for locations generally around and west of Interstate 65 through the Tennessee River Valley Region of Middle Tennessee. This resulted in a significant impact to commerce and transportation across this portion of the mid state, including several automobile accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 1/4 inch of ice accumulated in the higher elevations throughout Hickman County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved through the Mid-South January 15th into January 16th, 2013. Temperatures remained near or below freezing following another upper level disturbance that moved through on January 14th, 2013. As a result, precipitation from the disturbance that moved through on January 15th and 16th fell predominately in the form of freezing rain. Ice accumulations of a tenth to a quarter inch fell in many locations. However, most of the accumulations occurred on trees and power lines. Some of which were knocked down. Roads remained wet due to warm ground temperatures. Only bridges and overpasses became iced over due to them being elevated. In addition, colder air filtered into extreme Northwest Tennessee as the disturbance pushed east. This allowed freezing rain to change over to snow briefly before ending. Snow accumulations up to an inch occurred across Lake County. Some roads became snow covered as a result producing hazardous travel. One car accident occurred which produced one minor injury.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulations around a tenth of an inch fell across Obion County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a plume of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico streamed into Middle Tennessee during the afternoon through evening hours on Monday, January 14th, with sub-freezing temperatures near the surface across the mid state, ice accumulations due to freezing rainfall occurred, resulting in numerous automobile accidents and also a significant impact to commerce and transportation across the counties affected.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulations from freezing rain on several bridges in the Fairview area. This resulted in a significant impact to commerce and transportation during the late afternoon through evening hours in Fairview.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture overrunning a stalled frontal boundary created widespread freezing rain across the northwestern half of Middle Tennessee from the late evening hours on December 7 into the morning of December 8.  Since much of the ice accumulation that occurred on December 6 did not melt, these additional ice accumulations resulted in widespread tree damage and power outages across parts of northwest Middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Davidson County Emergency Management estimated around 0.10 inches of ice accumulation across most of the county, with 0.25 to 0.50 inches of ice accumulation at elevations above 900 feet. Ice formed on several bridges and overpasses across the county, resulting in several vehicle accidents. Ice on the 3rd Avenue Bridge over Interstate 65 just north of downtown Nashville contributed to a one vehicle accident with one fatality. Several trees and power lines were also knocked down in areas above 900 feet across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third winter weather event in as many days affected Middle Tennessee from late evening on December 9 into the morning of December 10.  Much of the southeastern half of Middle Tennessee experienced rain and some freezing rain, while parts of the northwestern half received a mix of rain, freezing rain, sleet, and snow. A narrow band of up to 1 inch of snow fell along a line from Lobelville to Nashville to Lafayette, causing numerous travel problems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported ice covered most elevated surfaces across the county.

In addition, colder air filtered into extreme Northwest Tennessee as the disturbance pushed east. This allowed freezing rain to change over to snow briefly before ending. Snow accumulations up to an inch occurred across Lake County. Some roads became snow covered as a result producing hazardous travel. One car accident occurred which produced one minor injury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third winter weather event in as many days affected Middle Tennessee from late evening on December 9 into the morning of December 10.  Much of the southeastern half of Middle Tennessee experienced rain and some freezing rain, while parts of the northwestern half received a mix of rain, freezing rain, sleet, and snow. A narrow band of up to 1 inch of snow fell along a line from Lobelville to Nashville to Lafayette, causing numerous travel problems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 1 inch of snow fell across Macon County. A CoCoRaHS observer measured 1.0 inch of snow in Siloam, and another CoCoRaHS observer measured 1.0 inch of snow 7 miles north-northeast of Red Boiling Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper level trough moved across the United States from Tuesday, January 29th into Wednesday, January 30th. This system contained unusually strong winds aloft, with wind speeds of 80 mph just 2500 feet above the surface and up to 150 mph at 20,000 feet. A record warm airmass with temperatures in the 60s and 70s spread northward ahead of the system into the Tennessee and Ohio Valleys, bringing weak atmospheric instability into the region. As a powerful cold front moved eastward across Middle Tennessee during the early morning hours of January 30, the strong low level jet ahead of the front caused strong gradient winds of 40 to 65 mph at the surface, resulting in several reports of wind damage. A peak sustained south wind of 45 mph with a peak gust of 64 mph was measured by the ASOS at the Nashville International Airport around 315 AM CST in association with these intense gradient winds. A line of showers and thunderstorms known as a Quasi-Linear Convective System (QLCS) developed along the front near the Tennessee River and raced eastward through the area between 2 AM and 6 AM, producing at least 23 tornadoes and widespread wind damage. This severe weather event resulted in 1 fatality and at least 3 injuries across the Mid State. The total of 23 tornadoes made the January 30, 2013 event the largest January tornado outbreak in Middle Tennessee history.  It also made January 30, 2013 the second biggest outbreak of tornadoes for any month in Middle Tennessee history.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado moved from Robertson County into Sumner County around one-half mile north of the Highway 25 and Highway 31 junction. Numerous trees were snapped or uprooted along Highway 31, Spring Valley Road, and South Road before the tornado lifted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a surface trough developed across Middle Tennessee during the morning hours on Friday, January 25th, it became a focusing mechanism for feezing rain development. This precipitation intially began as freezing rain, transitioning to rain from southern portions of the mid state to northern portions as the mid morning hours progressed. Freezing rain left ice accumulations generally around one to two tenths of an inch, resulting in numerous automobile accidents along with several trees and power poles being downed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mutiple automobile accidents reported over northern and western Davidson County due to ice accumulation from freezing rain of two tenths of an inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved through the Mid-South January 15th into January 16th, 2013. Temperatures remained near or below freezing following another upper level disturbance that moved through on January 14th, 2013. As a result, precipitation from the disturbance that moved through on January 15th and 16th fell predominately in the form of freezing rain. Ice accumulations of a tenth to a quarter inch fell in many locations. However, most of the accumulations occurred on trees and power lines. Some of which were knocked down. Roads remained wet due to warm ground temperatures. Only bridges and overpasses became iced over due to them being elevated. In addition, colder air filtered into extreme Northwest Tennessee as the disturbance pushed east. This allowed freezing rain to change over to snow briefly before ending. Snow accumulations up to an inch occurred across Lake County. Some roads became snow covered as a result producing hazardous travel. One car accident occurred which produced one minor injury.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulations around two tenths of an inch fell across Hardeman County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a plume of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico streamed into Middle Tennessee during the afternoon through evening hours on Monday, January 14th, with sub-freezing temperatures near the surface across the mid state, ice accumulations due to freezing rainfall occurred, resulting in numerous automobile accidents and also a significant impact to commerce and transportation across the counties affected.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automobile accident reported at bridge near mile marker one on Interstate 440 East due to ice accumulation from freezing rain. This location was also near West End Avenue. Specific details concerning amount and type of damage, associated repair costs, and if any injuries occurred, were unknown.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automobile accident on Interstate 65 at the Saturn Parkway intersection due to ice accumulation from freezing rain. Overpasses in this area reported as icy too due to ice accumulations from freezing rain. Specific details concerning amount and type of damage, associated repair costs, and if any injuries occurred, were unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a plume of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico streamed into Middle Tennessee during the afternoon through evening hours on Monday, January 14th, with sub-freezing temperatures near the surface across the mid state, ice accumulations due to freezing rainfall occurred, resulting in numerous automobile accidents and also a significant impact to commerce and transportation across the counties affected.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of automobile accidents reported across the county, including 10 reports on State Highway 840, due to ice accumulations from freezing rain. Specific details concerning amount and type of damage, associated repair costs, and if any injuries occurred, were unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified as it moved northeast along a stationary front stretching from Southwest Arkansas into the Ohio Valley. Deep moisture surged north from the Gulf of Mexico resulting in a large area of heavy showers and thunderstorms moving into western sections of the Mid-South during the evening of January 12, 2013. The showers and thunderstorms spread slowly east across the remainder of the Mid-South in advance of a cold front through the afternoon of January 13, 2013. The system dropped up to five inches of rain in isolated locations. The heavy rainfall resulted in some flash flooding and areal flooding across parts of the Mid-South.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced areal flooding across much of Hardeman County. Thirty-seven roads were flooded at the high point of the event. A couple of roads were washed out including Murphy Lane, Newton Lane and Peavine Road. Some residents of Murphy Lane were stranded for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and intense squall line moved across Middle Tennessee during the evening hours on December 21 and early morning hours on December 22, producing widespread wind damage in nearly every county of Middle Tennessee. Numerous trees and power lines were blown down with a few reports of damage to homes and businesses. Winds were measured up to 65 mph, and estimated as high as 70 mph in other areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down across Davidson County with numerous power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper level trough moved across the United States from Tuesday, January 29th into Wednesday, January 30th. This system contained unusually strong winds aloft, with wind speeds of 80 mph just 2500 feet above the surface and up to 150 mph at 20,000 feet. A record warm airmass with temperatures in the 60s and 70s spread northward ahead of the system into the Tennessee and Ohio Valleys, bringing weak atmospheric instability into the region. As a powerful cold front moved eastward across Middle Tennessee during the early morning hours of January 30, the strong low level jet ahead of the front caused strong gradient winds of 40 to 65 mph at the surface, resulting in several reports of wind damage. A peak sustained south wind of 45 mph with a peak gust of 64 mph was measured by the ASOS at the Nashville International Airport around 315 AM CST in association with these intense gradient winds. A line of showers and thunderstorms known as a Quasi-Linear Convective System (QLCS) developed along the front near the Tennessee River and raced eastward through the area between 2 AM and 6 AM, producing at least 23 tornadoes and widespread wind damage. This severe weather event resulted in 1 fatality and at least 3 injuries across the Mid State. The total of 23 tornadoes made the January 30, 2013 event the largest January tornado outbreak in Middle Tennessee history.  It also made January 30, 2013 the second biggest outbreak of tornadoes for any month in Middle Tennessee history.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS Storm Survey found straight line wind damage along Honeysuckle Road and Browning Branch Road northwest of Hartsville. Numerous trees were blown down and structural damage occurred to one home.  Numerous outbuildings and barns were also damaged. Winds were estimated around 80 mph.

As a powerful cold front moved eastward across Middle Tennessee during the early morning hours of January 30, the strong low level jet ahead of the front caused strong gradient winds of 40 to 65 mph at the surface, resulting in several reports of wind damage. A peak sustained south wind of 45 mph with a peak gust of 64 mph was measured by the ASOS at the Nashville International Airport around 315 AM CST in association with these intense gradient winds. A line of showers and thunderstorms known as a Quasi-Linear Convective System (QLCS) developed along the front near the Tennessee River and raced eastward through the area between 2 AM and 6 AM, producing at least 23 tornadoes and widespread wind damage. This severe weather event resulted in 1 fatality and at least 3 injuries across the Mid State. The total of 23 tornadoes made the January 30, 2013 event the largest January tornado outbreak in Middle Tennessee history.  It also made January 30, 2013 the second biggest outbreak of tornadoes for any month in Middle Tennessee history.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees were downed with power outages on Morris Road and Long Branch Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado began in Joelton along Harper Road between I-24 and Highway 41 where 3 large oak trees were uprooted and fell in a convergent pattern. The damage path continued east northeast with mostly tree damage. Along Bidwell Road, damage to a roof and barn was observed along with 10 to 15 downed trees. The path of damage crossed Wilkinson Road, Ridgewood Road, and Ivy Point Road where numerous trees were snapped and uprooted before the tornado lifted.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado moved out of Davidson County into Sumner County at Millersville. Numerous homes suffered minor roof and exterior damage along a narrow path across Williamson Road, Jesse Brown Drive, Cimmaron Drive, and other adjacent streets. Crossing I-65, a few more homes received minor roof damage on Lewis Drive. The tornado continued eastward with additional minor damage to homes along Patton Branch Road and Hogans Branch Road. Near the intersection of Tyree Springs Road and Sandy Valley Road, the tornado destroyed two garage buildings. Some more homes had minor roof damage along the path until the tornado lifted along Upper Station Camp Road. Dozens of trees were snapped and uprooted all along the path.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A second EF1 tornado touched down about 2.8 miles south-southwest of Cross Plains and moved northeast across eastern Robertson County and crossed Interstate 65 into western Sumner County. The tornado snapped or uprooted dozens of trees along Cross Plains Road north of Owens Chapel Road. A barn and mobile home were damaged along Jernigan Road, and several more trees were blown down on Calista Road. A home on Village Green Drive at Highway 25 was heavily damaged and a few others in the area suffered minor roof damage. More trees were snapped and uprooted as the tornado crossed Interstate 65 at mile marker 113 and Highway 31 one-half mile north of Highway 25 before the tornado moved into Sumner County. Winds were estimated around 95 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snow fell across the higher terrain in Southwest Virginia and Northeast Tennessee as well as the Smoky Mountains from Tuesday evening through Wednesday afternoon. The heavy snow event was the result of the passage of a deep upper level low pressure system. Much of these higher terrain areas picked up three to six inches of snow while the highest peaks in the Smoky Mountains recorded snow totals as high as ten to fifteen inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to five inches of snow was reported near Trade.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved through the Mid-South January 15th into January 16th, 2013. Temperatures remained near or below freezing following another upper level disturbance that moved through on January 14th, 2013. As a result, precipitation from the disturbance that moved through on January 15th and 16th fell predominately in the form of freezing rain. Ice accumulations of a tenth to a quarter inch fell in many locations. However, most of the accumulations occurred on trees and power lines. Some of which were knocked down. Roads remained wet due to warm ground temperatures. Only bridges and overpasses became iced over due to them being elevated. In addition, colder air filtered into extreme Northwest Tennessee as the disturbance pushed east. This allowed freezing rain to change over to snow briefly before ending. Snow accumulations up to an inch occurred across Lake County. Some roads became snow covered as a result producing hazardous travel. One car accident occurred which produced one minor injury.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A quarter of an inch of ice fell across McNairy County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a plume of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico continued streaming northeastward into Middle Tennessee during the afternoon hours on Tuesday, January 15th through the early morning hours on Wednesday, January 16th, and with surface temperatures near freezing, freezing rain that fell resulted in significant ice accumulations of between eight hundreths to one quarter of an inch for locations generally around and west of Interstate 65 through the Tennessee River Valley Region of Middle Tennessee. This resulted in a significant impact to commerce and transportation across this portion of the mid state, including several automobile accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were downed across the county due to ice accumulations of one tenth of an inch from freezing rain. This resulted in a significant impact to commerce and transportation across the county from the late afternoon hours on Tuesday, January 15th through the early morning hours on Wednesday, January 16th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tenth of an inch of ice accumulation from freezing rain resulted in some trees being downed on State Highway 96. This resulted in a significant impact to commerce and transportation in and around this area from the evening hours on Tuesday, January 15th through the early morning hours on Wednesday, January 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and intense squall line moved across Middle Tennessee during the evening hours on December 21 and early morning hours on December 22, producing widespread wind damage in nearly every county of Middle Tennessee. Numerous trees and power lines were blown down with a few reports of damage to homes and businesses. Winds were measured up to 65 mph, and estimated as high as 70 mph in other areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down with widespread power outages along Highway 50 near the Marshall County line.
