EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving upper level storm system brought a quick burst of light to moderate snow and sleet to the area. Snowfall amounts ranged from a dusting to two inches. The combination of light to moderate snow and sleet produced icy conditions on area roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 1 and 1.25 inches of snowfall was reported at Cowan, Tennessee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying surface low moved from the Southern Plains into the Great Lakes. Meanwhile the associated strong cold front swept across the Mid-South during the evening hours of January 29, 2013 into the early morning hours of January 30, 2013. Strong winds aloft combined with an increasingly unstable airmass set the stage for severe weather across the region. A broken line of thunderstorms moved into Eastern Arkansas during the evening hours of January 29, 2013 and eventually formed a solid line of thunderstorms as it crossed the Mississippi River and moved into West Tennessee and North Mississippi during the overnight hours.  There were numerous reports of damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds of 70 to 80 mph knocked down numerous trees and power lines on Rose Creek Road and Sulphur Springs Road in Western McNairy County. Large numbers of pine trees were pushed over or uprooted in Bethel Springs. A few of the trees were snapped and many power lines were knocked down.  An estimated 100 structures were damaged in McNairy County with the heaviest damage in Bethel Springs area.  A couple of vehicles were crushed by fallen trees in Bethel Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving upper level storm system brought a quick burst of light to moderate snow and sleet to the area. Snowfall amounts ranged from a dusting to two inches. The combination of light to moderate snow and sleet produced icy conditions on area roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of light snow and sleet resulted in a trace to just under 1/4 inch snow and sleet accumulation about four miles north-northeast of Huntland, Tennessee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front surged northward through North Mississippi into Southwest Tennessee during the evening hours of April 27th, 2013. As a result, several severe thunderstorms developed behind the front in the very unstable atmosphere. The storms produced damaging winds, large hail and flash flooding. One brief tornado also occurred as a severe thunderstorm crossed the warm front boundary. Elevated thunderstorms occurred ahead of the warm front. Some of these storms produced large hail and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding in Decatur County. Multiple places on Perryville Road west of McKenzie Road were impassable due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front surged northward through North Mississippi into Southwest Tennessee during the evening hours of April 27th, 2013. As a result, several severe thunderstorms developed behind the front in the very unstable atmosphere. The storms produced damaging winds, large hail and flash flooding. One brief tornado also occurred as a severe thunderstorm crossed the warm front boundary. Elevated thunderstorms occurred ahead of the warm front. Some of these storms produced large hail and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down just northeast of Exit 35 on Interstate 40 near Belmont. A large barn and several outbuildings were destroyed. The tornado tracked northeast producing damage to a rural church by pushing it 15 feet from its foundation. Numerous trees were snapped and uprooted along the damage path. The tornado lifted in a wooded area just to the north of Old 59 Drive. One minor injury occurred at a residence just before the tornado lifted.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds of 70 to 80 mph caused widespread damage across Southern Henderson County. Areas of enhanced damage was caused by thunderstorm winds estimated up to 110 mph. About 250 homes were damaged and several of them were destroyed. Numerous other structures were damaged. Numerous trees and power lines were knocked down. Three injuries were reported including one person that broke his hip.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving upper level storm system brought a quick burst of light to moderate snow and sleet to the area. Snowfall amounts ranged from a dusting to two inches. The combination of light to moderate snow and sleet produced icy conditions on area roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A half of an inch of snowfall was reported about three miles east-northeast of Huntland, Tennessee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving upper level storm system brought a quick burst of light to moderate snow and sleet to the area. Snowfall amounts ranged from a dusting to two inches. The combination of light to moderate snow and sleet produced icy conditions on area roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall accumulation of a half of an inch was reported on area roads and grassy surfaces, one mile east of Fayetteville, Tennessee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked through the region producing an increasing pressure gradient over the area.  As a result a high wind formed on the 10th. The highest wind  was reported across the higher elevation where wind gusts up to 85 mph were measured at the wind tower at Cove Mountain near Gatlinburg. Another gust of 85 mph was measured at the wind tower at the Camp Creek Elementary School in Camp Creek. The wind downed numerous trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement personnel reported a couple of trees downed by high wind 5 miles Townsend.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked through the region producing an increasing pressure gradient over the area.  As a result a high wind formed on the 10th. Another gust of 85 mph was measured at the wind tower at the Camp Creek Elementary School in Camp Creek. The wind downed numerous trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 85 mph was measured by the NOAA wind tower at the Camp Creek Elementary School in Camp Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-lived quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) tracked rapidly eastward across Tennessee through the early morning hours of the 30th.  Numerous tornadoes impacted middle Tennessee with this line, including a small EF-1 track in west central Lincoln County.  Very strong wind energy aloft was associated with this system along with an unseasonably warm and moist air mass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A short-lived tornado traveled slightly over 5 miles, impacting portions of west-central Lincoln County along the Elk River between the towns of Dellrose and Fayetteville, Tennessee.  The tornado initially touched down in a field just west of Cheatham Road, about a quarter of a mile south of Highway 273 (Old Elkton Pike), where several trees were snapped.  The tornado then quickly tracked northeast, flipping over a large irrigation system, uprooting and snapping several large cedar trees and blowing in two metal garage doors to a barn.||About one mile downstream, off JD Suggs Road, a 40x60-foot metal shed/barn was also completely destroyed.  Right along JD Suggs Road, a trailer was rolled on its side and significant roof and siding damage was done to an older barn.  Numerous large softwood trees were snapped near the base or uprooted around this location.  This is where the tornado appeared to be at its strongest, with maximum winds of around 100 mph and a maximum path width of around 250 yards.  Just northeast, along Curtis Road, additional trees were snapped and uprooted and another large irrigation system was turned over.  An attached garage/shed along the side of a residence collapsed with several large trees snapped and uprooted.  The tornado continued northeast  crossing the Elk River and snapping a few trees near the intersection of Highway 274 (Old Railroad Bed Road) and Hovis Bend Road. The side of a small barn was blown in and a portion of its roof was ripped off at a residence just up the road along Highway 274.  The was the last of any identifiable damage seen along this path as the tornado weakened and lifted over a field between Highway 274 and Dyer Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-lived quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) tracked rapidly eastward across Tennessee through the early morning hours of the 30th.  Numerous tornadoes impacted middle Tennessee with this line, including a small EF-1 track in west central Lincoln County.  Very strong wind energy aloft was associated with this system along with an unseasonably warm and moist air mass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A bridge on Beans Creek road was barricaded due to high water from heavy rain runoff, with several inches of water over the road. A weather observer in Winchester, Tennessee, about 11 miles northeast of the bridge, reported around three inches of rainfall. Radar estimates of two to four inches were also noted across the affected area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep area of low pressure moved into the region increasing the pressure gradient resulting in high non-thunderstorm wind over the area. The damaging wind was primarily across the higher elevations and foothills. Numerous trees and powerlines were downed by the wind.  A few buildings were also damaged.  Measured wind gusts of 71 mph was recorded at the Cove Mountain wind tower. In addition, a gust of 64 mph was measured at the wind tower at the UT Biology Field Station at Greenbrier.  Finally, a wind gust of 58 mph was measured at the wind tower at the Camp Creek Elementary School in Camp Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snow fell across the higher terrain in Southwest Virginia and Northeast Tennessee as well as the Smoky Mountains from Tuesday evening through Wednesday afternoon. The heavy snow event was the result of the passage of a deep upper level low pressure system. Much of these higher terrain areas picked up three to six inches of snow while the highest peaks in the Smoky Mountains recorded snow totals as high as ten to fifteen inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of snow was reported at Sam's Gap in the Pisgah National Forest at 3760 feet elevation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front surged northward through North Mississippi into Southwest Tennessee during the evening hours of April 27th, 2013. As a result, several severe thunderstorms developed behind the front in the very unstable atmosphere. The storms produced damaging winds, large hail and flash flooding. One brief tornado also occurred as a severe thunderstorm crossed the warm front boundary. Elevated thunderstorms occurred ahead of the warm front. Some of these storms produced large hail and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail at Highway 51 near Gates.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered flurries produced a dusting of snow at the University of the South at Sewanee, Tennessee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving upper level storm system brought a quick burst of light to moderate snow and sleet to the area. Snowfall amounts ranged from a dusting to two inches. The combination of light to moderate snow and sleet produced icy conditions on area roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A half of an inch of snow was reported mainly in grassy areas at Lynchburg, Tennessee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying surface low moved from the Southern Plains into the Great Lakes. Meanwhile the associated strong cold front swept across the Mid-South during the evening hours of January 29, 2013 into the early morning hours of January 30, 2013. Strong winds aloft combined with an increasingly unstable airmass set the stage for severe weather across the region. A broken line of thunderstorms moved into Eastern Arkansas during the evening hours of January 29, 2013 and eventually formed a solid line of thunderstorms as it crossed the Mississippi River and moved into West Tennessee and North Mississippi during the overnight hours.  There were numerous reports of damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds of 70 to 80 mph damaged 12 structures in Hardeman County including three homes and nine businesses including a lumber yard in Toone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying surface low moved from the Southern Plains into the Great Lakes. Meanwhile the associated strong cold front swept across the Mid-South during the evening hours of January 29, 2013 into the early morning hours of January 30, 2013. A broken line of thunderstorms moved into Eastern Arkansas during the evening hours of January 29, 2013 and eventually formed a solid line of thunderstorms as it crossed the Mississippi River and moved into West Tennessee and North Mississippi during the overnight hours.  There were numerous reports of damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds damaged about 100 homes across Chester County. A 33 foot long recreational vehicle was tossed into the family swimming pool and a 16 foot trailer was wrapped upside down around some trees about 200 yards away. The heaviest damage was in the northeast part of the county. Numerous trees and power lines were knocked down blocking several roads. A large barn was completely destroyed in the Jacks Creek area. Four people suffered minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance produced showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours of April 26, 2013. Small hail and flash flooding occurred over parts of West Tennessee. During the early morning hours of April 27, 2013 another upper level disturbance combined with an approaching warm front to produce additional showers and thunderstorms and isolated flash flooding across West Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in flash flooding across parts of Jackson. There was flooding along portions of Christmasville Road and at the corner of Skyline Drive and Summar Drive near the Jackson-Madison County General Hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a line of showers and thunderstorms moved west to east across Middle Tennessee during the morning hours of Monday, March 18th, some of these thunderstorms reached severe thresholds, resulting in large hail, thunderstorm wind damage, and the occurence of four different tornadoes. Three of these tornadoes were EF0 rated, with the fourth tornado rated an EF1 tornado, with a path length that went across three different counties. Two lightning strikes on school buildings across the mid state occurred also with these thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck in the area of the school's science and vocational buildings around 10 a.m.  Smoke was reported in part of the buildings, but firefighters said no fire was detected. Some electronic equipment was damaged, including the fire alarm system.|No injuries were reported, and students returned to classes within an hour of being evacuated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado touched down in northwestern Bedford County about 4.6 miles southwest of Unionville where a few trees were blown down and a barn damaged on Moon Bend Road. More trees were blown down and an outbuilding blown over on State Highway 270. A barn lost its roof and several more trees were snapped and uprooted in all directions on Clem Creek Road and Old Pencil Mill Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 Tornado with maximum sustained winds of 80 mph touched down on McKnight Road east of Culleoka where several trees were blown down and one mobile home had minor siding damage. A few more trees were blown down on Old Brush Creek Road. Numerous trees were snapped or uprooted in all directions along side roads north of Old Brush Creek Road. A few more trees were blown down on Joe Reeves Road and an old barn lost part of its roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across Middle Tennessee early on April 27 and continued into the afternoon hours as a weak warm frontal boundary was draped across the region. Some flooding was reported in the Nashville metro area. Additional showers and thunderstorms formed later in the day on April 27 and continued into April 28. Training thunderstorms caused widespread flash flooding. The worst flash flooding was across Houston, Stewart, and Montgomery counties where many homes and businesses were flooded, numerous roads were washed out, and several people were rescued from the flood waters. Several reports of wind damage and large hail were also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Repeated development of thunderstorms across Houston County resulted in 5 to 7 inches of rain falling during the evening hours on April 27. Major flash flooding resulted from the heavy rainfall, with several homes and businesses damaged along with numerous roadways flooded. A house off Midway Drive was significantly damaged by the flood. Erin First Baptist Church, Houston County Middle School, and Sudden Service were some of the non-residential buildings that flooded. Fourteen residents were evacuated from the Oak Brook Retirement Home. Roads that were flooded, closed, or suffered flood damage in Erin included Main Street, Mobley Lane, Mitchell Road, Spring Street, Metcalf Drive, Front Street, Denmark Road, Hill Street Bridge, Tank Hill Road, Maple Street, Highway 149 and Highway 147. Other roadways across the county that were flooded or received flood damage included Highway 147 in McKinnon, Dancer Branch Road, Herman Clark Road, Moore Branch Road, and Kaiser Ridge Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms formed along an outflow boundary across the Mid South and moved into the Southern Appalachians during the afternoon and early evening. The result of the early Spring event was mostly wind damage along with some small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down at Ross Campground in Church Hill.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down on HWY 41A (Clarksville Highway) at Rawlings Rd. in Joelton, TN. The tree was blocking north and southbound traffic on the highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper level low pressure system brought widespread rainfall to Middle Tennessee on May 4. Some localized flooding was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A water rescue of a family of four took place at a low water crossing on Mount Ararat Road. No injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front tracked through the area during the afternoon hours. Thunderstorms developed along and ahead of the front across east Tennessee. With the aid of moderate instability and strong winds a few of storms produced damaging thunderstorm wind.  Most of the damage was reported in Madisonville in Monroe County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement personnel reported one tree downed by thunderstorm wind in Pikeville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across Middle Tennessee early on April 27 and continued into the afternoon hours as a weak warm frontal boundary was draped across the region. Some flooding was reported in the Nashville metro area. Additional showers and thunderstorms formed later in the day on April 27 and continued into April 28. Training thunderstorms caused widespread flash flooding. The worst flash flooding was across Houston, Stewart, and Montgomery counties where many homes and businesses were flooded, numerous roads were washed out, and several people were rescued from the flood waters. Several reports of wind damage and large hail were also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate rainfall of 1 to 2 inches during the morning of April 27 was followed by heavy thunderstorms that produced an additional 2 to 4 inches of rainfall during the evening and into the early morning hours on April 28. The result was widespread flash flooding of roadways and a few homes across Davidson County, with numerous water rescues from people trapped in vehicles. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across Middle Tennessee early on April 27 and continued into the afternoon hours as a weak warm frontal boundary was draped across the region. Some flooding was reported in the Nashville metro area. Additional showers and thunderstorms formed later in the day on April 27 and continued into April 28. Training thunderstorms caused widespread flash flooding. The worst flash flooding was across Houston, Stewart, and Montgomery counties where many homes and businesses were flooded, numerous roads were washed out, and several people were rescued from the flood waters. Several reports of wind damage and large hail were also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Considerable flooding affected the Antioch area of southeast Davidson County on Saturday afternoon April 27 after radar estimates indicated 2 to 3 inches of rain had fallen since midnight. Locations along Mill Creek saw the most substantial flooding, such as Culbertson Road which was impassable due to high water, the Lighthouse Christian Ball Park along Blue Hole Road at I-24, and the old Family Fun Center on Bell Road at I-24.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two tornadoes developed from a cluster of thunderstorms during the early evening near Estill Springs TN.  The tornadoes developed as a smaller thunderstorm merged with a larger cluster of storms moving east.  The storms developed in a moist environment with weak to moderate shear in place, but far in advance of an upper level trough advancing through the lower Missouri Valley and Ozark regions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The same parent supercell storm that produced an earlier tornado in the county produced another brief tornado in the far northeast portion of the county.  The tornado touched down along Prairie Plains Road just north of Gum Creek Road.  Small trees were snapped and uprooted on the east side of the road.  A barn sustained minor roof and structural damage here as well.  Several trees were damaged near a homestead along Oak Grove Road.  The house here also had very minor roof damage.  The tornado lifted just northeast of Oak Grove Road in a very rural area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) arrived during the early morning hours of the 19th along a vigorous cold front. The environment was characterized by high wind shear and low instability. Several reports of wind damage and an isolated tornado occurred in portions of southern middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado first touched down just south of Highway 50/Fayetteville Highway, near Crystal Ridge Road, causing minor damage to a barn and snapping a few trees along Mulberry creek. The tornado continued to move east-northeast, crossing Gattistown road, snapping and uprooting both soft and hardwood trees. The tornado reached EF-1 intensity with maximum winds at 100 mph at this point along its track. The tornado then nearly paralleled Louse Creek road and moved into Moore county, Tennessee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) arrived during the early morning hours of the 19th along a vigorous cold front. The environment was characterized by high wind shear and low instability. Several reports of wind damage and an isolated tornado occurred in portions of southern middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The EF-1 tornado continued its track into Moore County, Tennessee, with damage observed to mainly soft and hardwood trees along Louse Creek road. The tornado then crossed Spencer Ridge road and Goose Branch road. Large hardwood trees were snapped off at the base as the tornado moved across the ridgeline. In this area, the tornado again reached its widest path of 400 yards and greatest intensity with winds estimated at 105 mph. As the tornado continued to move northeast, it crossed Highway 50/Winchester Highway. Large trees were downed in this area with a wide swath of power lines downed here. A telecommunications pole was also snapped near the top in this area. Tree damage was noted along Norman Weisman road with a roof peeled off of a large barn. A large tree was uprooted and fell onto the back of a house on Damron road with other large trees being uprooted in the vicinity. The tornado crossed Beech Hill road before lifting on the east side of Bakertown road.

Roadways were also flooded requiring the evacuation of homes near the I-24 and Highway 840 interchange in Murfreesboro, including Hardwood Drive, Nandina Court, and Camborne Circle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across Middle Tennessee early on April 27 and continued into the afternoon hours as a weak warm frontal boundary was draped across the region. Some flooding was reported in the Nashville metro area. Additional showers and thunderstorms formed later in the day on April 27 and continued into April 28. Training thunderstorms caused widespread flash flooding. The worst flash flooding was across Houston, Stewart, and Montgomery counties where many homes and businesses were flooded, numerous roads were washed out, and several people were rescued from the flood waters. Several reports of wind damage and large hail were also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Considerable flooding affected the La Vergne area of northwest Rutherford County on Saturday afternoon April 27 after radar estimates indicated 2 to 3 inches of rain had fallen since midnight. Locations along Mill Creek saw the most substantial flooding, such as Deer Drive where one home was flooded. Other homes on Taylor Drive and Apple Drive had water approach but not flood them.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two tornadoes developed from a cluster of thunderstorms during the early evening near Estill Springs TN.  The tornadoes developed as a smaller thunderstorm merged with a larger cluster of storms moving east.  The storms developed in a moist environment with weak to moderate shear in place, but far in advance of an upper level trough advancing through the lower Missouri Valley and Ozark regions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down along Luttrell Drive in Winchester just west of Estill Springs Road.  Large limbs were snapped off several trees.  Trees were uprooted at a farmstead on the west side of Old Estill Springs Road.  The tornado began to strengthen as it moved east northeast snapping the tops of trees along the edge of several farm fields.  A home on the west side of U.S. Highway 41 sustained roof damage with approximately 1/4 of its shingles removed.  In this area, several large diameter hardwood trees were snapped near the base.  Other large hardwood and softwood trees were uprooted in the vicinity.  The tornado reached its maximum intensity here with winds estimated at around 105 mph.  The tornado reached its maximum width of approximately 150 yards here.  As the tornado moved northeast, minor tree damage was noted along Asia Drive.  The tornado lifted on the west side of Highway 127 just south of Peabody Road.  Small softwood trees were snapped and uprooted here.  Investigations further east and northeast revealed no further continuous damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A high-end EF1 tornado with maximum winds around 105 mph and a path width of 150 yards that touched down in northeastern Humphreys County and crossed far southeastern Houston County continued moving northeastward before ending just southwest of Vanleer in western Dickson County at 0651 CST. One barn was destroyed and several others suffered roof damage on State Highway 46 north of Cedar Creek Road. Further to the east, one home completely lost its roof on Hill Road causing the collapse of the north exterior wall. However, the roof appeared to be poorly attached to the home. Debris from the home was blown over 100 yards to the|northeast. A poorly constructed single wide mobile home on Haliburton Road was blown 50 yards to the north into trees and completely destroyed. One barn was destroyed and others damaged further east on Haliburton Road. Dozens of trees were snapped or uprooted all along the track. Total path length of this tornado was 10.6 miles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across Middle Tennessee early on April 27 and continued into the afternoon hours as a weak warm frontal boundary was draped across the region. Some flooding was reported in the Nashville metro area. Additional showers and thunderstorms formed later in the day on April 27 and continued into April 28. Training thunderstorms caused widespread flash flooding. The worst flash flooding was across Houston, Stewart, and Montgomery counties where many homes and businesses were flooded, numerous roads were washed out, and several people were rescued from the flood waters. Several reports of wind damage and large hail were also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Repeated development of thunderstorms across Stewart County resulted in 5 to 7 inches of rain falling during the evening hours on April 17. Disastrous flash flooding resulted from the heavy rainfall, with numerous roadways washed out or underwater, dozens of homes flooded, and five water rescues of people in vehicles. The worst flooding occurred in the Indian Mound and Carlisle communities, where 20 people were trapped by flood waters. One man suffered minor injuries after driving off a washed out section of Leatherwood Road, with his car falling ten feet down into a ravine. Other flooded and washed out roadways across Stewart County included Taylor's Chapel Road, Rorie Hollow, North Cross Creek Road, Big Rock Road, Joiner Hollow Road, Robertson Hill Road, Wildlife Road, Norris Handcock Road, Centerpoint Road, Indian Mound Road, and Highway 49. In total, 25 homes were reported destroyed, 25 homes suffered major damage, and 20 homes had minor damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Orographics and a slightly to moderately unstable atmosphere appeared to play a role in severe thunderstorms that formed on the Cumberland Plateau across Southeast Tennessee as well as in the communities of Oak Ridge and Knoxville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Road 302 closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) developed over the lower Mississippi Valley and tracked eastward into the Tennessee Valley during the evening and overnight hours of the 21st into the 22nd.  Ahead of the line, windy conditions developed with south winds gusting to over 40 mph at times.  The thunderstorms and showers produced strong winds at times, producing a few reports of tree damagel.  Rainfall of 1 to 2 inches was common across the entire area.  As the band of heavier showers and storms slowed down over southern middle Tennessee, rainfall amounts increased over 3 inches in a few locations.  Small creeks and streams experienced rapid rises for a short time period, but no significant river flooding was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were downed by thunderstorm winds along Highway 231 at Chestnut Ridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of light freezing drizzle and rain developed during the pre-dawn hours in southern middle Tennessee just in advance of a cold front.  Up to a tenth of an inch of ice accumulated on elevated surfaces in Moore and Franklin Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to a tenth of an inch of icing was reported on fences at the Fayetteville Tennessee Municipal Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong lifting of a moist air mass in the presence of an upper level low pressure system resulted in a heavy wet snowfall event during the period from around noon est until early evening. Atmospheric dynamics were so intense at times, that lightning was generated; i.e.  thundersnow. Much of the snow accumulated across the region from Central East Tennessee northeast through Southwest Virginia, generally north of Interstate 40 and east of Interstate 75. The upper level low moved east from Northern to Northeast Georgia generating 3 to 5 inches of snow across the Great Valley of Central East Tennessee northeast to the Tri-Cities area. Much greater snowfall amounts occurred across Southwest Virginia with enhancement in the higher terrain in this region as well as over the mountains of Northeast Tennessee. In these areas, snowfall amounts ranged from 6 inches to as much as a foot in the highest elevations across Southwest Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four inches of snow was reported three miles north of Morristown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The arctic cold front that brought ice, sleet and snow accumulations on December 5th and December 6th stalled along the Gulf Coast on December 7th, 2013. Low pressure developed along the front and spread precipitation northward into West Tennessee during the evening of December 7th into the early morning hours of December 8th. Since arctic air was already in place, precipitation fell in the from of freezing rain and sleet. Anywhere from a tenth of an inch to a quarter of an inch fell across West Tennessee, mainly southeast of a Ripley to Martin line. Bridges and overpasses became iced and several accidents occurred as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulations were around a tenth of an inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front tracked through the area triggering isolated thunderstorms over east Tennessee during the overnight hours.  With the aid of a strong low-level wind field a few of the storms produced a damaging wind downing some trees and powerlines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager personnel reported a few trees and powerlines downed by thunderstorm wind 11 miles northwest of Wartburg in Deer Lodge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture overrunning a stalled frontal boundary created widespread freezing rain across the northwestern half of Middle Tennessee from the late evening hours on December 7 into the morning of December 8.  Since much of the ice accumulation that occurred on December 6 did not melt, these additional ice accumulations resulted in widespread tree damage and power outages across parts of northwest Middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media reports indicated 0.10 inches of ice accumulation in Westmoreland. Ice accumulations of 0.10 to 0.25 inches were estimated across Sumner County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front tracked through the area triggering isolated thunderstorms over east Tennessee during the overnight hours.  With the aid of a strong low-level wind field a few of the storms produced a damaging wind downing some trees and powerlines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked through the region producing an increasing pressure gradient over the area.  As a result a high wind formed on the 10th. The highest wind  was reported across the higher elevation where wind gusts up to 85 mph were measured at the wind tower at Cove Mountain near Gatlinburg. Another gust of 85 mph was measured at the wind tower at the Camp Creek Elementary School in Camp Creek. The wind downed numerous trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Park service personnel reported several trees downed by high wind along Forge Creek Road 8 miles southwest of Townsend in Cades Cove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a slow moving cold front moved west to east across Middle Tennesssee during the late morning through afternoon hours on Sunday, January 13th, an area of showers and thunderstorms developed along and ahead of this cold front. Because of the slow movement of this cold front, the heavier showers and thunderstorms produced flash flooding in five counties across the mid state, with another two counties experiencing thunderstorm wind damage and a funnel cloud respectively.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple roads were closed throughout the county.  Also a telephone pole was downed on State Highway 10 North.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a plume of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico continued streaming northeastward into Middle Tennessee during the afternoon hours on Tuesday, January 15th through the early morning hours on Wednesday, January 16th, and with surface temperatures near freezing, freezing rain that fell resulted in significant ice accumulations of between eight hundreths to one quarter of an inch for locations generally around and west of Interstate 65 through the Tennessee River Valley Region of Middle Tennessee. This resulted in a significant impact to commerce and transportation across this portion of the mid state, including several automobile accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automobile accidents due to ice accumulations of one tenth of an inch due from  freezing rain on Fort Campbell Boulevard, two miles northwest of Clarksville. Bridges and overpasses across this area were also icy. Specific details concerning how many, amount and type of damage, and associated repair costs with these automobile accidents, were unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a plume of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico continued streaming northeastward into Middle Tennessee during the afternoon hours on Tuesday, January 15th through the early morning hours on Wednesday, January 16th, and with surface temperatures near freezing, freezing rain that fell resulted in significant ice accumulations of between eight hundreths to one quarter of an inch for locations generally around and west of Interstate 65 through the Tennessee River Valley Region of Middle Tennessee. This resulted in a significant impact to commerce and transportation across this portion of the mid state, including several automobile accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around five miles south of Nashville, about 0.25 of an inch of ice accumulation due to freezing rain occurred on trees and roads signs around the Oak Hill Area of the county. Large tree limbs were downed as well. This resulted in a significant impact to commerce and transportation in and around this area from the evening hours on Tuesday, January 15th through the early morning hours on Wednesday, January 16th. Also reported was that the road over Percy Priest Dam located in eastern Davidson County was also frozen over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a strong upper level low passed south of Middle Tennesssee as the day progressed on Thursday, Janurary 17th, rainfall during the morning hours changed over to sleet snow mix, resulting in accumulating amounts across these two counties. This caused a significant impact to commerce and transportation across these counties, including closure or postponements of schools and civic engagements across these counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch of total snowfall accumulation occurred on average across the county.|Numerous automobile accidents also occurred across the county. This total snowfall accumulation resulted in school and civic engagement closures or postponements across the county, as travel on roads across the county became hazardous due to the accumulating snowfall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automobile accidents with unknown number of injuries at South James Campbell Boulevard and Brookmeade Drive due to slick roads from a sleet and snow mix.|Specific details concerning amount and type of damage, associated repair costs, and the exact amount of injuries that occurred, were unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain returned to the region. Though not as heavy as the event of the 15th-16th, the very wet soils enabled this event to produce widespread flooding as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bridge washout on Carrs Creek Road. Low area of Old Walland Hwy along the river had two feet of water on it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant synoptic scale event produced several inches of rain over much of the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front triggered high non-thunderstorm winds across the mountains.  Gusts as high as 89mph at Cover Mountain near Gatlinburg.  Also, a wind gust of 78mph was measured at the NOAA wind tower at the Camp Creek Elementary School at Camp Creek.  Gusts up to 60mph were also reported at the at the UT Field Station in Greenbrier.  In addition, a gust up to 55mph was recorded at Look Rock near Maryville.  Also a gust up to 50mph was reported at Cades Cove near Townsend.   Most of the wind damage was reported at Smoky National Park where multiple trees were downed on the roads and 2 vehicles in the park where damaged by the falling trees.  The rest of the area had trees and powerline downed  by the high wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement personnel reported high wind caused power outages along Crackers Neck Road 6 miles south-southwest of Mountain City.

Another gust of 85 mph was measured at the wind tower at the Camp Creek Elementary School in Camp Creek. The wind downed numerous trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong lifting of a moist air mass in the presence of an upper level low pressure system resulted in a heavy wet snowfall event during the period from around noon est until early evening. Atmospheric dynamics were so intense at times, that lightning was generated; i.e.  thundersnow. Much of the snow accumulated across the region from Central East Tennessee northeast through Southwest Virginia, generally north of Interstate 40 and east of Interstate 75. The upper level low moved east from Northern to Northeast Georgia generating 3 to 5 inches of snow across the Great Valley of Central East Tennessee northeast to the Tri-Cities area. Much greater snowfall amounts occurred across Southwest Virginia with enhancement in the higher terrain in this region as well as over the mountains of Northeast Tennessee. In these areas, snowfall amounts ranged from 6 inches to as much as a foot in the highest elevations across Southwest Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 4.1 inches was reported two miles south southwest of Jonesborough.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The arctic cold front that brought ice, sleet and snow accumulations on December 5th and December 6th stalled along the Gulf Coast on December 7th, 2013. Low pressure developed along the front and spread precipitation northward into West Tennessee during the evening of December 7th into the early morning hours of December 8th. Since arctic air was already in place, precipitation fell in the from of freezing rain and sleet. Anywhere from a tenth of an inch to a quarter of an inch fell across West Tennessee, mainly southeast of a Ripley to Martin line. Bridges and overpasses became iced and several accidents occurred as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around a quarter of an inch of ice fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third winter weather event in as many days affected Middle Tennessee from late evening on December 9 into the morning of December 10.  Much of the southeastern half of Middle Tennessee experienced rain and some freezing rain, while parts of the northwestern half received a mix of rain, freezing rain, sleet, and snow. A narrow band of up to 1 inch of snow fell along a line from Lobelville to Nashville to Lafayette, causing numerous travel problems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported ice covered most elevated surfaces across the county with ice on some bridges and overpasses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture overrunning a stalled frontal boundary created widespread freezing rain across the northwestern half of Middle Tennessee from the late evening hours on December 7 into the morning of December 8.  Since much of the ice accumulation that occurred on December 6 did not melt, these additional ice accumulations resulted in widespread tree damage and power outages across parts of northwest Middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter and media reports indicated numerous trees and power lines down across Robertson County with scattered power outages mainly in southern part of the county.  Several trees were knocked down on power lines along Empson Drive in Greenbrier. An NWS employee in White House measured overnight ice accumulations of 0.13 inches. Overnight ice accumulations up to 0.25 inches brought total ice accumulations from December 6 to December 8 up to 0.25 to 0.50 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) developed over the lower Mississippi Valley and tracked eastward into the Tennessee Valley during the evening and overnight hours of the 21st into the 22nd.  Ahead of the line, windy conditions developed with south winds gusting to over 40 mph at times.  The thunderstorms and showers produced strong winds at times, producing a few reports of tree damagel.  Rainfall of 1 to 2 inches was common across the entire area.  As the band of heavier showers and storms slowed down over southern middle Tennessee, rainfall amounts increased over 3 inches in a few locations.  Small creeks and streams experienced rapid rises for a short time period, but no significant river flooding was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few nine inch diameter tree limbs were knocked down onto Spring Creek Road just north of Estill Springs, Tennessee.

Up to a quarter inch of ice accumulated in far southern portions of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper level trough moved across the United States from Tuesday, January 29th into Wednesday, January 30th. This system contained unusually strong winds aloft, with wind speeds of 80 mph just 2500 feet above the surface and up to 150 mph at 20,000 feet. A record warm airmass with temperatures in the 60s and 70s spread northward ahead of the system into the Tennessee and Ohio Valleys, bringing weak atmospheric instability into the region. As a powerful cold front moved eastward across Middle Tennessee during the early morning hours of January 30, the strong low level jet ahead of the front caused strong gradient winds of 40 to 65 mph at the surface, resulting in several reports of wind damage. A peak sustained south wind of 45 mph with a peak gust of 64 mph was measured by the ASOS at the Nashville International Airport around 315 AM CST in association with these intense gradient winds. A line of showers and thunderstorms known as a Quasi-Linear Convective System (QLCS) developed along the front near the Tennessee River and raced eastward through the area between 2 AM and 6 AM, producing at least 23 tornadoes and widespread wind damage. This severe weather event resulted in 1 fatality and at least 3 injuries across the Mid State. The total of 23 tornadoes made the January 30, 2013 event the largest January tornado outbreak in Middle Tennessee history.  It also made January 30, 2013 the second biggest outbreak of tornadoes for any month in Middle Tennessee history.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado began near Green River Road just southeast of Highway 13 and moved eastward, blowing down dozens of trees along Phillips Chapel Road and Harris Road. 2 barns were heavily damaged and another barn was completely destroyed at a farm on the east end of Harris Road. Dozens of trees continued to be snapped and uprooted along the path as the tornado continued eastward into Lewis County. Aerial and street view imagery from Google Earth greatly assisted with this storm survey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system moved across the Tennessee & Ohio Valleys during the afternoon and evening hours on October 31. A very strong southerly low level jet, measured at 82 knots at 850mb on the OHX November 1 00Z upper air sounding, caused strong to damaging gradient winds at the surface of 50 to 60 mph for many hours west of the Cumberland Plateau.  Scattered showers and thunderstorms also developed during the evening hours as a cold front swept across Middle Tennessee, with several reports of damaging winds received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees, power lines, and power poles were blown down across Robertson County. One power pole was snapped and fell on a car at a car dealership in Ridgetop. An above ground pool was destroyed in Cross Plains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front which was partly responsible for a large tornado and severe weather outbreak across the Ohio Valley and eastern Corn Belt dropped  into southern middle Tennessee during the evening hours of the 17th.  Along the front, a broken line of low-topped showers produced a brief EF-0 tornado in Lincoln County in the Howell community.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief EF-0 tornado with peak winds up to 85 mph touched down just west of Haysland Road, about 6 miles northwest of Fayetteville near Howell.  The front porch of a small one-story residence was lifted up, taking with it, a large portion of a metal roof.  Debris from the roof and porch was thrown about 50 yards across Haysland Road.  Two small cedar trees were snapped (one near its base) just north of the home.  Just east of this point across Haysland Road, two smaller trees were snapped near their bases and a small portion of vinyl siding was stripped along the windward facing side of a two-story house.||The tornado brieflly lifted before producing additional damage near the intersection of Brown Teal Road and March Mill Road.  At one residence off Brown Teal Road, the roof from a small wood barn was completely lifted and thrown in several directions.  A two by four  piece of wood became a projectile and smashed into the house, leaving a small hole.  At the next house east (corner of Brown Teal Road and March Mill Road), several Bradford pear trees (up to 1 foot in diameter) were snapped near the base.  A carport attached to the southwest side of a single wide manufactured home was lifted and crushed.  A few metal roof panels from this home were lifted off or peeled back.  Some of this debris was thrown several hundred yards east of this point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front triggered high non-thunderstorm wind across the mountains.  Gusts were as high as 89mph at Cover Mountain near Gatlinburg.  Also, a wind gust of 78mph was measured at the NOAA wind tower at the Camp Creek Elementary School at Camp Creek.  Gusts up to 60mph were also reported at the UT Field Station in Greenbrier. In addition, park service personnel reported a 55 mph wind gust measured at Look Rock as well as a 50 mph gust at Cades Cove.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust of 60 mph was recorded at the observation site at the UT Field Station in Greenbrier 2 miles east of Gatlinburg.

One 9 year old boy was killed by electrocution around 930 PM CDT when he rode his bicycle over a live power line that had been knocked down by a fallen large tree limb. This death occurred in the 500 block of Charles E Davis Boulevard around 1.5 miles southeast of downtown Nashville. Trees were reportedly blown down in Old Hickory, a large tree limb was blown down on power lines in the Charlotte Park area of west Nashville, and a large tree limb was blown down in Sevier Park. Trees were blown down and blocked roads such as Robertson Avenue in west Nashville, Towry Street at Paragon Mills Road in Antioch, and Carter Avenue in east Nashville. Two houses were set on fire in Oak Hill at Rochelle Drive and Overton Drive due to downed power lines. A few windows were broken out of the Omni Hotel in downtown Nashville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper level trough moved across the United States from Tuesday, January 29th into Wednesday, January 30th. This system contained unusually strong winds aloft, with wind speeds of 80 mph just 2500 feet above the surface and up to 150 mph at 20,000 feet. A record warm airmass with temperatures in the 60s and 70s spread northward ahead of the system into the Tennessee and Ohio Valleys, bringing weak atmospheric instability into the region. As a powerful cold front moved eastward across Middle Tennessee during the early morning hours of January 30, the strong low level jet ahead of the front caused strong gradient winds of 40 to 65 mph at the surface, resulting in several reports of wind damage. A peak sustained south wind of 45 mph with a peak gust of 64 mph was measured by the ASOS at the Nashville International Airport around 315 AM CST in association with these intense gradient winds. A line of showers and thunderstorms known as a Quasi-Linear Convective System (QLCS) developed along the front near the Tennessee River and raced eastward through the area between 2 AM and 6 AM, producing at least 23 tornadoes and widespread wind damage. This severe weather event resulted in 1 fatality and at least 3 injuries across the Mid State. The total of 23 tornadoes made the January 30, 2013 event the largest January tornado outbreak in Middle Tennessee history.  It also made January 30, 2013 the second biggest outbreak of tornadoes for any month in Middle Tennessee history.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado touched down south of Hohenwald along Wildwood Road and moved eastward through rural forested areas before lifting east of Highway 20 to the south of Springer Church House Road. A mobile home had minor roof damage on Swiss Colony Road. 1 mobile home was completely destroyed, 5 mobile homes were damaged, and an outbuilding was destroyed on the south end of Kayla Lane. Another mobile home lost its roof on the east end of Colonial Road and 2 others were damaged. A house on Highway 20 just south of Grinder Creek Road suffered minor roof damage. Hundreds of trees were snapped and uprooted all along the path. Aerial and street view imagery from Google Earth greatly assisted with this storm survey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper level trough moved across the United States from Tuesday, January 29th into Wednesday, January 30th. This system contained unusually strong winds aloft, with wind speeds of 80 mph just 2500 feet above the surface and up to 150 mph at 20,000 feet. A record warm airmass with temperatures in the 60s and 70s spread northward ahead of the system into the Tennessee and Ohio Valleys, bringing weak atmospheric instability into the region. As a powerful cold front moved eastward across Middle Tennessee during the early morning hours of January 30, the strong low level jet ahead of the front caused strong gradient winds of 40 to 65 mph at the surface, resulting in several reports of wind damage. A peak sustained south wind of 45 mph with a peak gust of 64 mph was measured by the ASOS at the Nashville International Airport around 315 AM CST in association with these intense gradient winds. A line of showers and thunderstorms known as a Quasi-Linear Convective System (QLCS) developed along the front near the Tennessee River and raced eastward through the area between 2 AM and 6 AM, producing at least 23 tornadoes and widespread wind damage. This severe weather event resulted in 1 fatality and at least 3 injuries across the Mid State. The total of 23 tornadoes made the January 30, 2013 event the largest January tornado outbreak in Middle Tennessee history.  It also made January 30, 2013 the second biggest outbreak of tornadoes for any month in Middle Tennessee history.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado began around 1 mile west-southwest of Hurricane Mills and moved eastward before lifting 4 miles east-northeast of town. Approximately 500 trees were snapped or uprooted along the path of the tornado through Hurricane Mills. A barn was also destroyed and several homes and other buildings suffered significant roof damage at the Loretta Lynn Ranch and Campground area. The tornado crossed Highway 13 and continued eastward along Stewart Branch Road, with hundreds more trees snapped and uprooted. The tornado lifted west of Bold Spring Road. Aerial imagery from Google Earth greatly assisted with this storm survey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front tracked through the area triggering thunderstorms across east Tennessee during the afternoon hours.  A few of the storms produced a damaging thunderstorm wind downing some trees.  Hail as large as a quarter was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway department personnel reported 2-3 trees downed by thunderstorm wind countywide.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on a survey completed by Coffee County Emergency Management, an EF0 tornado was confirmed in and near Manchester. The sheet metal roof of a home was partially blown off on Walker Road in Manchester and a tree was blown down in the yard. A few trees were blown down and the press box was destroyed at the Coffee County Middle School.  Intermittent damage consisting of dozens of snapped trees and damaged roofs continued for approximately 5 miles to the northeast.  The last evidence of damage was on Shining Star Lane just off Ragsdale Road where a home had minor damage and a few trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a slow moving cold front moved west to east across Middle Tennesssee during the late morning through afternoon hours on Sunday, January 13th, an area of showers and thunderstorms developed along and ahead of this cold front. Because of the slow movement of this cold front, the heavier showers and thunderstorms produced flash flooding in five counties across the mid state, with another two counties experiencing thunderstorm wind damage and a funnel cloud respectively.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS damage survey team confirmed that an EF0 tornado touched down Sunday afternoon just southwest of Eagleville in Rutherford County. Initial damage was noted on Floyd road approximately 2 miles southwest of Eagleville, where several cedar trees were snapped. A residence along Floyd road suffered partial roof loss along with a window and door blown out. An older outbuilding on Floyd road was flattened. Approximately 100 trees were snapped and uprooted along the approximate 1.5 mile track of the tornado.

Rainfall totals ranging from 2 to 8 inches resulted along a narrow swath including northern Nashville, Whites Creek, Bordeaux, Inglewood, and Goodlettsville. *INSERT MORE INFO HERE*.

There were dozens of water rescues across the city, including along Interstate 24 and Briley Parkway where several motorists had to be rescued.  All told, Nashville OEM responded to over 200 calls for help from residents.

A bulk of the rainfall occurred during the late morning through late afternoon hours. Lincoln county was particularly hard hit where 5 to 10 inches of rain were measured. In addition to the flash flooding, widespread areal flooding occurred and numerous roads were closed or became impassible due to high water in some areas through the evening of July 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 110 near Kirkland had around a foot of flowing water over both lanes. Ditches were full.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance produced showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours of April 26, 2013. Small hail and flash flooding occurred over parts of West Tennessee. During the early morning hours of April 27, 2013 another upper level disturbance combined with an approaching warm front to produce additional showers and thunderstorms and isolated flash flooding across West Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding in Brighton. Old Highway 51 was covered with eight to ten inches of water near the Brighton Elementary School. Other roads in Tipton County also were covered by several inches of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low moved southeastward across Middle Tennessee during the afternoon hours on June 10. Moderate wind shear combined with the cold mid level temperatures with the upper level low to produce numerous supercell thunderstorms and small bow echoes across mainly central portions of Middle Tennessee, with widespread wind damage and a few large hail reports. Many of the supercell thunderstorms had spectacular visual structure with well-defined wall clouds and shelf clouds. This was the largest severe weather event to affect Middle Tennessee in the meteorological Summer of 2013.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees and power lines were blown down onto Southeast Broad Street around one-quarter mile south of Middle Tennessee Boulevard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of squall lines moved through mostly eastern Middle Tennessee during the afternoon hours resulting in damaging winds, hail, flash flooding, and damaging lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down at the intersection of Dickens Hill Road and Highway 64 in Bradyville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on Tyree Springs Road near White House High School.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain developed along a stalled front across Northeast Arkansas early in the day causing flash flooding. This heavy rain eventually sagged into Northwest Tennessee resulting in some flash flooding there later in the day. As a day progressed a potent upper level disturbance moved out of the East Texas while a surface low pressure system tracked from Arkansas along the stalled front. Moist and unstable air moved into the Mid-South in advance of the stalled front and the developing low pressure system. As a result a line of showers and thunderstorms moved across West Tennessee during the evening hours of December 21st.  Several storms were severe with reports of damaging winds, hail and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell at Shelby Drive and Forest Hill Irene Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three separate squall line/bowing segments began impacting the Middle Tennessee area around mid day and ending around midnight. These systems produced mostly damaging straight line winds with some hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A roof was blown off a house on Plunk Whitson Road in the Double Springs community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system moved across the Tennessee & Ohio Valleys during the afternoon and evening hours on October 31. A very strong southerly low level jet, measured at 82 knots at 850mb on the OHX November 1 00Z upper air sounding, caused strong to damaging gradient winds at the surface of 50 to 60 mph for many hours west of the Cumberland Plateau.  Scattered showers and thunderstorms also developed during the evening hours as a cold front swept across Middle Tennessee, with several reports of damaging winds received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down in Hilham.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and intense squall line moved across Middle Tennessee during the evening hours on December 21 and early morning hours on December 22, producing widespread wind damage in nearly every county of Middle Tennessee. Numerous trees and power lines were blown down with a few reports of damage to homes and businesses. Winds were measured up to 65 mph, and estimated as high as 70 mph in other areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down with roof damage to several homes on Ashburn Road from the 5500 block to Fred Perry Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying surface low moved from the Southern Plains into the Great Lakes. Meanwhile the associated strong cold front swept across the Mid-South during the evening hours of January 29, 2013 into the early morning hours of January 30, 2013. Strong winds aloft combined with an increasingly unstable airmass set the stage for severe weather across the region. A broken line of thunderstorms moved into Eastern Arkansas during the evening hours of January 29, 2013 and eventually formed a solid line of thunderstorms as it crossed the Mississippi River and moved into West Tennessee and North Mississippi during the overnight hours.  There were numerous reports of damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding in parts of Tipton County. Brighton-Clopton Road was flooded near Brighton. A few homes on Hall Road were flooded with up to one foot of water. Walnut Grove Road and Gay Road were flooded near Burlison.
